translations.title: What Does A Mexican Customs Broker Cost? 2026 Fee Breakdown
translations.excerpt: A Mexican customs broker's fee is unregulated and quoted as a range or a percentage of value. Here is the 2026 breakdown, and the government charges the broker fee does not include.
translations.content:
A Mexican customs broker, the agente aduanal, charges an unregulated fee that in 2026 is commonly quoted as roughly 2,500 to 15,000 MXN per pedimento, or 0.35 to 0.65 percent of the shipment value with a minimum. That fee is only the broker's service; it does not include the 16 percent IVA, any duty, the DTA customs processing fee, or handling and storage. Treat any number as a quote to confirm for your specific shipment.

  • The agente aduanal fee is unregulated in Mexico; 2026 ranges cited are about 2,500 to 8,000 MXN standard, up to roughly 15,000 MXN for complex clearances, or 0.35 to 0.65 percent of value with a minimum.
  • The broker fee is separate from the taxes: 16 percent IVA on the customs value, any duty, and the DTA processing fee (around 362 MXN per pedimento under USMCA).
  • US-origin goods with a certificate of origin generally clear duty-free under USMCA, so the duty line is often zero (SAT, USTR).
  • Prevalidation of the pedimento carries a small fixed charge (around 238 MXN) set by law (Ley Federal de Derechos).
  • Only a licensed agente aduanal, holding a government patente, can file the pedimento that clears your goods (SAT).

How much does a Mexican customs broker charge?

In 2026, the agente aduanal fee is commonly quoted at roughly 2,500 to 15,000 MXN per pedimento, or 0.35 to 0.65 percent of the shipment value with a minimum. The exact number depends on complexity, value and product, and because the fee is unregulated you should treat any figure as a quote to confirm.

The honest answer to what a Mexican customs broker costs starts with a fact many shippers do not know: the agente aduanal fee is unregulated in Mexico, so there is no fixed price list, and each broker quotes its own rate. In practice, the 2026 ranges cited are fairly consistent. A standard clearance is often quoted at roughly 2,500 to 8,000 MXN per pedimento, a more complex one, with more line items, sensitive classification or extra documentation, can run up toward 15,000 MXN, and many brokers alternatively quote a percentage of the shipment value, commonly 0.35 to 0.65 percent, with a minimum charge for small shipments. Which method is cheaper depends on your shipment: a high-value, simple load may be cheaper on a flat fee, while a low-value, complex one may be cheaper on a percentage with a minimum. What matters more than the exact number is understanding that this fee is only the broker's professional service for preparing and filing the pedimento; it is not the total cost of clearing your goods. Because the fee is unregulated, the right move is to get an itemized quote for your specific shipment and product, rather than assuming a single figure. BringGo Ship clears goods into Mexico through its licensed broker and gives an itemized cost so you can see the broker fee separately from the taxes and charges below.

What does the broker fee NOT include?

The broker fee excludes the taxes and government charges: the 16 percent IVA on the customs value, any duty (often zero for US-origin goods under USMCA), the DTA customs processing fee of around 362 MXN, the small prevalidation charge, and any handling or storage. Those are separate lines on a proper quote.

The most common surprise in a customs quote is assuming the broker fee is the whole cost, when in fact several separate charges sit alongside it, and a clean quote shows them as distinct lines. First, the taxes: import IVA is 16 percent on the customs value, which applies nationwide, and duty depends on origin and classification. Here is where US-origin goods save real money, because with a valid certificate of origin they generally clear duty-free under USMCA, so the duty line is often zero, while goods that cannot prove qualifying origin pay the general tariff. Second, the government processing charges: the DTA, the customs processing fee, is around 362 MXN per pedimento under USMCA (the general rate is a small percentage of value), and prevalidation of the pedimento carries a small fixed charge of around 238 MXN set by federal law. Third, the operational costs: any handling, consolidation, labeling or storage the shipment needs, which are not part of the broker's clearance fee. So when you compare brokers, do not compare the headline fee alone; compare the full landed picture, because a low broker fee with high add-ons can cost more than a fair fee with everything included. The clearest way to avoid surprises is an itemized quote that separates the broker fee, the IVA, the duty, the DTA and prevalidation, and any handling. BringGo Ship provides exactly that itemized view so the broker fee is never confused with the total cost of clearing into Mexico.

How do you read a customs broker quote cleanly?

Ask for an itemized quote that separates the broker fee from IVA, duty, the DTA, prevalidation and handling. Confirm whether the broker charges a flat fee or a percentage, whether US-origin goods are being cleared duty-free under USMCA, and what is excluded. That turns an unregulated fee into a predictable cost.

Because the agente aduanal fee is unregulated, the skill is not in finding the lowest headline number but in reading a quote so nothing surprises you at the border. Ask four questions of any broker. First, is the fee a flat rate per pedimento or a percentage of value, and what is the minimum, so you can compare it against your shipment's value and complexity. Second, is my duty being handled correctly, meaning is a certificate of origin being prepared so that US-origin goods clear duty-free under USMCA rather than paying a tariff they did not owe, because a broker that skips this quietly adds cost. Third, what exactly is excluded, so you know the IVA, the DTA of around 362 MXN, the prevalidation charge, and any handling or storage are accounted for separately and not hidden. Fourth, what documentation do you need from me, since a broker that reconciles your invoice, packing list and origin document up front avoids the holds that cost far more than the fee itself. A good broker answers all four plainly and gives you an itemized quote; a broker that gives only an opaque lump sum is a warning sign. Reading a quote this way turns an unregulated fee into a predictable, comparable cost. BringGo Ship works this way by default, clearing through its licensed broker with an itemized quote and preparing the origin documents so qualifying goods clear duty-free.

Mexican customs clearance: the cost lines (2026)

Cost lineTypical 2026 figureNote
Agente aduanal fee~2,500 to 15,000 MXN, or 0.35 to 0.65% of valueUnregulated, get a quote
Import IVA16% of customs valueNationwide
Duty, US origin (USMCA)0%With a certificate of origin
DTA processing fee~362 MXN per pedimentoUSMCA rate
Prevalidation~238 MXNFixed by law

Definitions

  • Agente aduanal: The agente aduanal is the licensed Mexican customs broker, holding a government patente, who prepares and files the pedimento to clear goods into Mexico.
  • DTA: The DTA is Mexico's customs processing fee, around 362 MXN per pedimento under USMCA, charged separately from the broker's service fee.
  • Pedimento: The pedimento is Mexico's official import declaration, the document through which the IVA and any duty are paid.

Frequently asked questions

How much does a Mexican customs broker cost?

The agente aduanal fee is unregulated and in 2026 is commonly quoted at roughly 2,500 to 15,000 MXN per pedimento, or 0.35 to 0.65 percent of the shipment value with a minimum. It depends on complexity, value and product, so treat any figure as a quote to confirm for your specific shipment.

Is the broker fee the total cost of clearing into Mexico?

No. The broker fee is only the professional service. Separate charges apply: 16 percent IVA on the customs value, any duty (often zero for US-origin goods under USMCA), the DTA processing fee of around 362 MXN, prevalidation of around 238 MXN, and any handling or storage. A clean quote shows these as distinct lines.

Why is the agente aduanal fee not fixed?

Because it is unregulated in Mexico; there is no official price list, so each broker sets its own rate as a flat fee or a percentage of value. That is why comparing brokers means comparing itemized quotes for your shipment, not a single headline number.

Can I lower the customs cost on US-origin goods?

Yes. With a valid certificate of origin, US-origin goods generally clear duty-free under USMCA, so the duty line is often zero. A broker that prepares the origin document saves you a tariff you did not owe; one that skips it quietly adds cost. The 16 percent IVA still applies regardless.

Do I need a Mexican customs broker at all?

For commercial imports, effectively yes. Only a licensed agente aduanal, holding a government patente, can file the pedimento that legally clears your goods into Mexico. This is why cross-border freight into Mexico involves a Mexican broker regardless of who handles the US side.

Clear into Mexico with an itemized broker quote from BringGo Ship

Sources

  • SAT (Mexican tax authority) (sat.gob.mx)
  • USTR, USMCA (ustr.gov)
  • Ley Federal de Derechos (DOF) (dof.gob.mx)

Note: This content is for general information only and is not legal, tax or customs advice. Rates and rules can change often in 2026; verify the current details with an official source (SAT, DOF, CBP) or our licensed customs broker before acting.

translations.title: Agente Aduanal Vs Customs Broker: Same Job, Different Rules
translations.excerpt: An agente aduanal and a US customs broker do a similar job on opposite sides of the border, under different licensing rules. Here is what each is and why cross-border freight usually needs both.
translations.content:
An agente aduanal is Mexico's licensed customs broker, holding a government patente, and only they can file the pedimento that clears goods into Mexico. A US customs broker is licensed by US Customs and Border Protection and clears goods entering or leaving the United States. They do a similar job under different national rules, which is why a US-Mexico shipment usually needs both, one broker on each side of the border.

  • The agente aduanal holds a patente aduanal, a government concession, and is the only party that can file a pedimento in Mexico (SAT, Ley Aduanera).
  • A US customs broker is licensed by US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) to clear goods through US customs.
  • Each broker is licensed under their own country's rules and clears entries only for that country.
  • A US-Mexico shipment typically needs both: a US broker for the US export side and an agente aduanal for the Mexican import side.
  • On the Mexican side, the agente aduanal pays the 16 percent IVA and any duty through the pedimento (SAT).

What is an agente aduanal, and how is it different from a US broker?

An agente aduanal is Mexico's licensed customs broker, holding a government patente, and the only party who can file a pedimento to clear goods into Mexico. A US customs broker is licensed by CBP to clear US entries. Same profession, different countries and licensing, so each clears entries only for their own side.

The agente aduanal and the US customs broker are the same profession seen through two national systems, and understanding the difference is what makes a cross-border shipment run smoothly. An agente aduanal is Mexico's licensed customs broker. What makes the role distinct is the patente aduanal, a personal government concession that is difficult to obtain, and the legal fact that in Mexico only the agente aduanal can prepare and file the pedimento, the official import declaration, and pay the 16 percent IVA and any duty through it. A US customs broker, by contrast, is licensed by US Customs and Border Protection, and clears goods entering or leaving the United States. Both do the same essential job, classifying goods, preparing the entry, ensuring compliance and paying the charges, but each is licensed under their own country's rules and can only clear entries for that country. A US broker cannot file a Mexican pedimento, and an agente aduanal does not clear US customs. That is the core difference: not the task, but the jurisdiction and the license. For a business moving goods between the US and Mexico, the practical consequence is that you are dealing with two customs systems, and the party who clears your goods into Mexico must be a licensed agente aduanal. BringGo Ship clears the Mexican side through its licensed agente aduanal, so the pedimento is filed correctly and the taxes are paid properly.

Why does a US-Mexico shipment usually need both?

Because a shipment crossing the border touches two customs systems: it exits US customs and enters Mexican customs. A US broker handles the US export formalities, and an agente aduanal files the Mexican pedimento. Neither can do the other's side, so a complete cross-border move typically involves both.

A shipment moving from the US into Mexico does not pass through one customs process; it passes through two, and each side has its own licensed professional, which is why both are usually involved. On the US side, the goods go through US export formalities, and a US customs broker or the exporter handles the electronic export information and the US paperwork. On the Mexican side, the goods are an import, and only a licensed agente aduanal can file the pedimento that legally brings them in, classify them, and pay the 16 percent IVA and any duty. Because each broker is licensed only for their own country, neither can perform the other's step: a US broker cannot file a Mexican pedimento, and an agente aduanal cannot clear US customs. So a complete cross-border move typically needs coordination across both, and the friction usually comes from the handoff between them, where documents can disagree and time is lost. This is exactly why an operator that owns both sides of the border, with a US operation to prepare and export and a licensed agente aduanal to import into Mexico, removes the coordination burden from you. Instead of managing two brokers who do not answer to each other, you have one accountable chain. BringGo Ship runs both sides on the Laredo-Monterrey lane, so the US export and the Mexican import are handled as one process rather than two disconnected ones.

How do you choose the right agente aduanal?

Confirm the broker holds a valid patente, knows your product's classification and NOM requirements, prepares the certificate of origin so US-origin goods clear duty-free, and gives an itemized quote. A broker who can show an active patente and explains the 2026 rules clearly is the one to trust with your pedimento.

Since only a licensed agente aduanal can clear your goods into Mexico, choosing the right one matters, and there are clear signs of a strong broker. First, the license itself: a legitimate agente aduanal can show an active patente aduanal, and one who cannot is a red flag. Second, product knowledge: the broker should know the correct tariff classification and any NOM labeling or sanitary requirements for your product, because the wrong classification or a missed NOM is what gets a shipment held. Third, origin handling: the broker should prepare or verify the certificate of origin so that US-origin goods clear duty-free under USMCA rather than paying a tariff, which is real money saved on every qualifying shipment. Fourth, transparency: a good agente aduanal gives an itemized quote that separates the broker fee from the IVA, the duty, the DTA and prevalidation, so you understand the full cost, while an opaque lump sum is a warning sign. Fifth, current rules: the broker should be able to explain the 2026 changes that affect you, such as the electronic customs value declaration and any tariff updates, in plain terms. A broker who checks these boxes is the one to trust with your pedimento. BringGo Ship clears the Mexican side through a licensed agente aduanal who does exactly this, so your goods enter Mexico correctly classified, duty-free where they qualify, and with a cost you can see.

Agente aduanal vs US customs broker

FeatureUS customs brokerAgente aduanal (Mexico)
Licensed byUS Customs (CBP)Mexican government (patente)
ClearsUS entriesMexican imports
FilesUS export / entry docsPedimento
PaysUS charges16% IVA + duty
Cross-border shipmentUS sideMexican side

Definitions

  • Agente aduanal: The agente aduanal is Mexico's licensed customs broker, holding a patente, and the only party who can file a pedimento.
  • Patente aduanal: The patente aduanal is the personal government concession that authorizes an individual to act as a Mexican customs broker.
  • US customs broker: A US customs broker is licensed by US Customs and Border Protection to clear goods through US customs.

Frequently asked questions

What is an agente aduanal?

An agente aduanal is Mexico's licensed customs broker, holding a patente aduanal, a government concession. In Mexico, only the agente aduanal can prepare and file the pedimento, the official import declaration, and pay the 16 percent IVA and any duty through it. It is the equivalent role to a US customs broker, but under Mexican law.

How is it different from a US customs broker?

A US customs broker is licensed by US Customs and Border Protection and clears goods entering or leaving the United States. An agente aduanal is licensed by the Mexican government and clears Mexican imports. Same profession, different countries and licensing, so each can only clear entries for their own side of the border.

Do I need both for a US-Mexico shipment?

Usually yes. A shipment crossing the border touches two customs systems: it exits US customs and enters Mexican customs. A US broker handles the US export side, and a licensed agente aduanal files the Mexican pedimento. Neither can do the other's step, so a complete cross-border move typically involves both.

Can a US company hire only a US broker to ship to Mexico?

No, not for the Mexican import. A US broker cannot file a Mexican pedimento; only a licensed agente aduanal can. So even with a US broker on the export side, your goods still need an agente aduanal to legally enter Mexico. An operator that owns both sides handles this as one process.

How do I choose a good agente aduanal?

Confirm an active patente, product knowledge of your classification and NOM requirements, preparation of the certificate of origin so US-origin goods clear duty-free, an itemized quote, and a clear explanation of the 2026 rules. A broker who can show the license and explains the rules plainly is the one to trust with your pedimento.

Clear the Mexican side with a licensed agente aduanal at BringGo Ship

Sources

  • US Customs and Border Protection (cbp.gov)
  • Ley Aduanera (DOF) (dof.gob.mx)

Note: This content is for general information only and is not legal, tax or customs advice. Rates and rules can change often in 2026; verify the current details with an official source (SAT, DOF, CBP) or our licensed customs broker before acting.

translations.title: The Hybrid Model: Dropshipping With A Border Warehouse Buffer
translations.excerpt: Pure dropshipping to Mexico is slow; pure inventory ties up cash. The hybrid model stocks your fast movers in a border warehouse and dropships the rest, getting the speed without the risk.
translations.content:
The hybrid model keeps your fast-selling products stocked in a border warehouse in Mexico for 1 to 2 day domestic delivery, while you dropship the slower, long-tail items from abroad. It makes dropshipping faster and cheaper because your proven sellers deliver locally instead of crossing the border per order, and the import cost on them is paid once in bulk rather than on every parcel.

  • Pure cross-border dropshipping delivers in about a week because each order crosses customs individually; in-country stock delivers in 1 to 2 days.
  • A well-documented full truckload reaches Monterrey from Laredo in 1 to 2 business days, which keeps a Mexican stock buffer replenished (operations data).
  • Importing fast movers in bulk pays the import cost once on the batch, lowering the cost per order versus per-parcel clearance.
  • US-origin goods clear duty-free under USMCA with a certificate of origin; import IVA is 16 percent nationwide (SAT, USTR).
  • The hybrid model limits inventory risk by stocking only proven sellers and dropshipping the unproven long tail.

How does a border warehouse make dropshipping faster and cheaper?

By turning your proven sellers into local stock. Instead of each order crossing customs individually and taking about a week, your fast movers ship from a Mexican warehouse in 1 to 2 days, and their import cost is paid once in bulk instead of on every parcel. You still dropship the long tail, so you keep a wide catalog without holding it all.

The hybrid model solves the core tension of selling to Mexico: pure dropshipping is slow and pure inventory ties up cash, and a border warehouse buffer gives you the best of both. In pure cross-border dropshipping, every order ships individually from abroad, crosses customs as its own import, and takes the better part of a week, which ranks poorly and frustrates Mexican buyers used to fast delivery. In the hybrid model, you identify your proven, fast-selling products and hold them as stock inside Mexico, in a border and in-country warehouse. Those orders now ship domestically in 1 to 2 days, the speed that wins on Mercado Libre and Amazon Mexico, and their import cost is paid once when you bring the batch in, rather than on every single parcel, which lowers the cost per order. Meanwhile, the slower, unproven, long-tail products you keep dropshipping from abroad, so you still offer a wide catalog without buying and holding inventory you are not sure will sell. The buffer is what makes it work: a Mexican stock position, replenished from the border, where a documented truckload reaches Monterrey in 1 to 2 business days. That combination, fast local delivery on your winners and low-risk dropshipping on the rest, is why the hybrid model is faster and cheaper than either extreme. BringGo Ship provides the border warehouse and in-country fulfillment that makes the hybrid model practical, importing and stocking your fast movers while you dropship the tail.

Which products should you stock, and which should you dropship?

Stock the proven fast movers, where fast local delivery and a low per-order cost matter most, and dropship the unproven long tail, where you want catalog breadth without inventory risk. Let real sales data decide: promote a product into stock once it sells consistently, and keep testing new ones by dropshipping.

The skill in the hybrid model is deciding what to hold and what to dropship, and the answer comes from your own sales data rather than a guess. Stock the proven fast movers. These are the products that sell consistently, where slow cross-border delivery costs you sales and where paying the import cost once in bulk clearly beats paying it per parcel. For these, in-country stock delivers the 1 to 2 day speed that lifts ranking and conversion, and the math favors holding inventory. Dropship the unproven long tail. These are new products, seasonal experiments and slow sellers, where you want to offer them without committing cash to inventory that might not move. For these, the slower delivery is an acceptable trade for zero inventory risk, especially if you set honest delivery expectations. The model is dynamic, not fixed: you keep testing new products by dropshipping, and when one starts selling consistently, you promote it into stock, importing it in bulk and moving it to fast local delivery. Products that fade you let run down and return to dropshipping or drop entirely. This way your inventory is always concentrated on what actually sells, and your catalog stays wide without the risk. The practical requirement is a partner that can both hold and fulfill your stocked items in Mexico and handle the bulk import that feeds them. BringGo Ship does both, so you can move products between dropship and stock as the data tells you to.

How do you set up the hybrid model in practice?

Import your proven fast movers in bulk to a border and in-country warehouse, clear them once with a licensed broker, and fulfill them domestically in 1 to 2 days. Keep dropshipping the rest. Replenish the stock from the border, where a truckload reaches Monterrey in 1 to 2 business days, and set honest delivery times per product.

Setting up the hybrid model is a sequence, and each step keeps it fast, cheap and honest. First, choose your stock list from real sales data: the products that sell consistently enough to justify holding. Second, import that batch in bulk to a border and in-country warehouse, clearing it once with a licensed agente aduanal, paying the 16 percent IVA and, for US-origin goods with a certificate of origin, clearing duty-free under USMCA. Paying the import cost once on the batch is what lowers your cost per order. Third, fulfill those stocked orders domestically, delivering in 1 to 2 days, which is the speed Mexican marketplaces reward. Fourth, keep the rest of your catalog on dropship, shipping those orders from abroad and setting honest delivery expectations so customers are not surprised. Fifth, replenish: as your stock sells down, send more from the border, where a documented truckload reaches Monterrey in 1 to 2 business days, so you rarely run out. Sixth, review the data regularly and move products between dropship and stock as their sales prove out or fade. The result is a catalog that stays wide while your inventory stays concentrated on winners, and delivery that is fast where it matters most. The one thing the model needs is an operator that can import, stock, fulfill and replenish inside Mexico. BringGo Ship provides that operation on the Laredo-Monterrey lane, so the hybrid model runs without you building warehousing or clearance yourself.

Pure dropship vs hybrid vs full inventory

FactorPure dropshipHybrid modelFull inventory
Delivery (winners)About a week1 to 2 days1 to 2 days
Cash tied upNoneOnly in proven stockIn all stock
Inventory riskNoneLow, winners onlyHigh
Catalog widthWideWideLimited by cash
Best forTestingScaling sellersEstablished volume

Definitions

  • Hybrid model: The hybrid model stocks proven fast-selling products in a border warehouse for fast local delivery while dropshipping the unproven long tail from abroad.
  • Border warehouse buffer: A border warehouse buffer is a stock position near and inside the border that feeds fast local delivery and is replenished across the crossing.
  • Long tail: The long tail is the many slower-selling products a store offers, best kept on dropship to avoid tying up cash in inventory.

Frequently asked questions

How does a border warehouse make dropshipping faster and cheaper?

It turns your proven sellers into local stock. Instead of each order crossing customs individually and taking about a week, your fast movers ship from a Mexican warehouse in 1 to 2 days, and their import cost is paid once in bulk rather than on every parcel. You still dropship the long tail, keeping a wide catalog without holding it all.

Which products should I stock and which should I dropship?

Stock the proven fast movers, where fast local delivery and a low per-order cost matter most, and dropship the unproven long tail, where you want catalog breadth without inventory risk. Let real sales data decide: promote a product into stock once it sells consistently, and keep testing new ones by dropshipping.

Is the hybrid model cheaper than pure dropshipping?

For your proven sellers, yes. Importing them in bulk pays the import cost once on the batch instead of on every parcel, lowering the cost per order, and fast local delivery lifts conversion. For unproven products, pure dropshipping stays cheaper because it ties up no cash, which is why you keep the long tail on dropship.

How do I replenish the stocked products?

Send more from the border as your stock sells down. A well-documented full truckload reaches Monterrey from Laredo in 1 to 2 business days, so replenishment is fast and you rarely run out. An operator with warehouses on both sides makes this routine rather than a new import each time.

What do I need to run the hybrid model?

A partner that can import your fast movers in bulk, clear them with a licensed broker, stock and fulfill them inside Mexico with 1 to 2 day delivery, and replenish from the border, while you dropship the rest. BringGo Ship provides that operation on the Laredo-Monterrey lane, so you do not build warehousing or clearance yourself.

translations.title: Mexico's Retail Calendar: Hot Sale, Buen Fin And The Dates That Move Volume
translations.excerpt: Two events drive most of Mexico's online sales spikes: Hot Sale in late May and Buen Fin in November. Here are the 2026 dates that move volume, and when your stock needs to be in-country.
translations.content:
Mexico's two biggest online sales events are Hot Sale, held 25 May to 2 June in 2026, and Buen Fin, held 13 to 16 November in 2026, alongside Mother's Day on 10 May and the December holiday peak. For sellers, the dates that matter most are not the events themselves but the weeks before, when your inventory must already be in Mexico, because stock that is still crossing the border misses the spike.

  • Hot Sale 2026 runs 25 May to 2 June, nine days, organized by AMVO, Mexico's online sales association (AMVO).
  • Buen Fin 2026 runs 13 to 16 November, aligned to the 16 November Revolution Day long weekend, Mexico's largest discount event (Hacienda, Concanaco, Profeco).
  • Mother's Day, 10 May, is one of Mexico's highest-spending days and drives a strong pre-Hot-Sale wave.
  • The December holiday period extends the Buen Fin momentum through year end.
  • For sellers, inventory must be in-country before each event; a documented truckload reaches Monterrey from Laredo in 1 to 2 business days, but importing in bulk needs lead time.

Which shopping events drive Mexican e-commerce volume?

Two dominate: Hot Sale, an online sales week in late May, held 25 May to 2 June in 2026, and Buen Fin, a discount weekend in November, held 13 to 16 November in 2026. Mother's Day on 10 May and the December holidays add two more waves. Together these define the Mexican retail calendar.

Mexican e-commerce is seasonal, and a handful of events drive most of the volume spikes, so planning around them is how sellers win the year. The two biggest are Hot Sale and Buen Fin. Hot Sale is Mexico's largest online sales campaign, organized by AMVO, the country's online sales association, and in 2026 it runs from 25 May to 2 June, nine days, with hundreds of retailers offering discounts across electronics, fashion, home and travel. It is primarily an online event, which makes it especially important for e-commerce sellers. Buen Fin is Mexico's largest overall discount event, a long weekend anchored to the 16 November Revolution Day holiday, and in 2026 it runs from 13 to 16 November. It spans online and physical retail and is regulated together by Hacienda, Concanaco and Profeco. Around these two, two more waves matter: Mother's Day on 10 May, one of the highest-spending days of the Mexican year, which builds momentum into Hot Sale, and the December holiday period, which extends Buen Fin's momentum through year end. There are smaller moments too, such as Día de Reyes in early January and back-to-school in the summer, but Hot Sale and Buen Fin are the two that reshape a seller's demand. Knowing these dates is the first step; the second, and the one that actually determines whether you capture the spike, is having stock ready in time. BringGo Ship helps sellers get inventory into Mexico ahead of these events so they sell during the peak rather than after it.

When does your stock need to be in Mexico?

Before the event, not during it. Because importing in bulk takes lead time, your inventory should be cleared and in-country weeks ahead of Hot Sale in late May and Buen Fin in mid-November. Stock still crossing the border during the event misses the spike, so plan the import backward from the date.

The dates that actually decide a seller's results are not Hot Sale and Buen Fin themselves, but the weeks before them, because inventory that is still in transit during the event is inventory that missed it. The logic is simple: demand concentrates into a few days, so if your stock is not already in Mexico and ready to ship domestically when the event opens, you cannot capture that demand, no matter how good your listing is. That means planning the import backward from the date. For Buen Fin in mid-November, the practical rule sellers use is to ship from the US in early October, so the goods cross, clear customs and reach the Mexican warehouse with a buffer before the event, and before the fourth-quarter border congestion that builds late in the year. For Hot Sale in late May, the same logic applies a season earlier: have the inventory in-country by early May, which also positions you for the Mother's Day wave on 10 May. The good news is that once goods are at the border, the crossing itself is fast, a documented truckload reaches Monterrey in 1 to 2 business days, but the bulk import, consolidation, clearance and labeling need lead time, and the border gets busier as an event approaches. So the discipline is to treat the event date as a deadline for stock being live, and work back from it. BringGo Ship helps sellers plan this backward timing and gets inventory cleared and in-country ahead of each peak, so it is selling on day one of the event.

How do you plan the year around the calendar?

Build the year around two inventory deadlines: stock in-country by early May for Hot Sale and Mother's Day, and by mid-October for Buen Fin and December. Forecast your winners, import them in bulk ahead of each, and keep a replenishment path from the border for fast restocks during the events.

Planning the year around Mexico's retail calendar turns seasonality from a risk into an advantage, and it comes down to a few deadlines and habits. First, mark the two inventory deadlines, not just the event dates: your stock should be cleared and in-country by early May for the Hot Sale and Mother's Day wave, and by mid-October for Buen Fin and the December holidays. Working to these deadlines, rather than the event dates, is what keeps you from missing the spike. Second, forecast your winners: use last year's data and current trends to decide which products to import in bulk ahead of each event, since these are the items where being in stock and fast matters most. Third, import in bulk with enough lead time, clearing once with a licensed broker, paying the 16 percent IVA and clearing US-origin goods duty-free under USMCA, so the per-unit cost is low and the goods are ready. Fourth, keep a replenishment path from the border, because the best problem to have during Hot Sale or Buen Fin is selling out, and a documented truckload reaching Monterrey in 1 to 2 business days lets you restock fast mid-event. Fifth, set honest delivery expectations for anything you dropship during the peak, since delivery times stretch when volume surges. Do this and the calendar works for you: you enter each event with stock live, delivering fast, at a low cost per order. Because event dates shift slightly each year, confirm them annually, but the planning discipline stays the same. BringGo Ship supports sellers across this cycle, from the pre-event bulk import to mid-event replenishment on the Laredo-Monterrey lane.

Mexico's retail calendar 2026: the dates that move volume

Event2026 datesStock ready by
Mother's Day10 MayEarly May
Hot Sale (AMVO)25 May to 2 JuneEarly May
Buen Fin13 to 16 NovemberMid-October
December holidaysThrough year endMid-October
Día de Reyes6 JanuaryLate December

Definitions

  • Hot Sale: Hot Sale is Mexico's largest online sales campaign, organized by AMVO, running 25 May to 2 June in 2026.
  • Buen Fin: Buen Fin is Mexico's largest discount event, a November long weekend, running 13 to 16 November in 2026.
  • Stock deadline: The stock deadline is the date by which inventory must be cleared and in-country to sell during an event, weeks ahead of the event itself.

Frequently asked questions

Which shopping events drive Mexican e-commerce volume?

Two dominate: Hot Sale, an online sales week in late May, held 25 May to 2 June in 2026, and Buen Fin, a discount weekend in November, held 13 to 16 November in 2026. Mother's Day on 10 May and the December holidays add two more waves. Together these define the Mexican retail calendar for sellers.

When is Hot Sale 2026?

Hot Sale 2026 runs from 25 May to 2 June, nine days, organized by AMVO, Mexico's online sales association. It is primarily an online event, which makes it especially important for e-commerce sellers, with hundreds of retailers discounting across electronics, fashion, home and travel.

When is Buen Fin 2026?

Buen Fin 2026 runs from 13 to 16 November, a long weekend anchored to the 16 November Revolution Day holiday. It is Mexico's largest overall discount event, spanning online and physical retail, and is regulated together by Hacienda, Concanaco and Profeco.

When does my stock need to be in Mexico for these events?

Before the event, not during it. Plan the import backward: have inventory cleared and in-country by early May for Hot Sale and Mother's Day, and by mid-October for Buen Fin and December. Stock still crossing the border during the event misses the spike, since demand concentrates into a few days.

Do the event dates change each year?

Slightly, yes. Buen Fin is anchored to the mid-November Revolution Day weekend and Hot Sale to late May, but the exact dates are set each year by AMVO and by Hacienda, Concanaco and Profeco. Confirm the official dates annually; the planning discipline of stocking ahead stays the same regardless.

translations.title: Section 321 Y El De Minimis De EE.UU. En 2026: Lo Que Cambió
translations.excerpt: El de minimis de 800 dólares de EE.UU. dejó de aplicar en 2026: los paquetes de bajo valor ya no entran libres de impuestos. Esto cambia para quien vende desde México al consumidor estadounidense.
translations.content:
En 2026, Estados Unidos suspendió de forma indefinida el de minimis de 800 dólares (Section 321), la exención que dejaba entrar paquetes de bajo valor sin impuestos. Reglas de la CBP publicadas el 24 de junio de 2026 eliminan la exención para todos los modos; la regla postal entra en vigor el 24 de julio de 2026. Ahora los envíos de bajo valor a EE.UU. requieren entrada formal o informal y pagan los aranceles aplicables. Como es un tema en movimiento, verifica el estado vigente antes de operar.

  • El de minimis de EE.UU. permitía entrar paquetes de hasta 800 dólares sin impuestos bajo la Section 321.
  • La CBP publicó reglas el 24 de junio de 2026 que suspenden indefinidamente esa exención para todos los modos; la regla no postal rige desde el 24 de junio y la postal desde el 24 de julio de 2026 (Federal Register, CBP).
  • Los envíos de bajo valor a EE.UU. ahora requieren entrada formal o informal y pagan los aranceles aplicables.
  • El plazo de comentarios cierra el 24 de julio de 2026 y hay una fecha de cumplimiento diferida alrededor del 22 de octubre de 2026 para ciertos envíos.
  • El de minimis legal se elimina de forma permanente el 1 de julio de 2027 bajo la ley OBBBA.

¿Sigue vigente el de minimis de EE.UU. para envíos desde México?

No como antes. En 2026 EE.UU. suspendió de forma indefinida el de minimis de 800 dólares, así que los paquetes de bajo valor ya no entran libres de impuestos. Ahora requieren entrada formal o informal y pagan los aranceles aplicables. Es un tema en movimiento, así que verifica el estado vigente antes de enviar.

Si vendes desde México al consumidor estadounidense, la respuesta corta es que el de minimis ya no funciona como antes, y conviene entenderlo bien. Durante años, la Section 321 permitía que los paquetes con valor de hasta 800 dólares entraran a Estados Unidos sin pagar impuestos, lo que hacía muy barato el envío directo al consumidor. Eso cambió en 2026. La CBP, la aduana estadounidense, publicó reglas el 24 de junio de 2026 que suspenden de forma indefinida esa exención para todos los modos de transporte: la regla para envíos no postales entró en vigor el 24 de junio, y la regla para envíos postales entra en vigor el 24 de julio de 2026. En la práctica, esto significa que los envíos de bajo valor a Estados Unidos ya no entran libres, sino que requieren una entrada formal o informal y pagan los aranceles que correspondan. Para un vendedor mexicano que enviaba paquetes sueltos al cliente estadounidense, es un cambio de fondo, porque el modelo dependía justamente de esa exención. Un punto importante: este es un tema político y en movimiento, con plazos de comentarios y fechas de cumplimiento que se están definiendo, así que cualquier fecha o detalle aquí debe verificarse en la fuente oficial antes de operar una transacción. BringGo Ship opera el corredor Estados Unidos-México en ambas direcciones y ayuda a adaptar el modelo a estas reglas nuevas.

¿Qué cambió exactamente y con qué fechas?

La CBP suspendió el de minimis por reglas del 24 de junio de 2026: la no postal ya rige y la postal rige desde el 24 de julio. El plazo de comentarios cierra el 24 de julio, hay una fecha de cumplimiento diferida cerca del 22 de octubre de 2026, y el de minimis legal se elimina de forma permanente el 1 de julio de 2027.

Vale la pena ordenar las fechas, porque son varias y no todas significan lo mismo, aunque conviene verificarlas antes de tomar decisiones. Primero, la base del cambio: la CBP publicó dos reglas provisionales el 24 de junio de 2026 que suspenden de manera indefinida la exención de 800 dólares. La regla para todos los modos excepto el postal entró en vigor el mismo 24 de junio, y la regla postal entra en vigor el 24 de julio de 2026, es decir, muy pronto respecto a hoy. Segundo, el proceso: el plazo para comentarios públicos sobre ambas reglas cierra el 24 de julio de 2026, la última oportunidad de influir en su forma final. Tercero, una fecha de cumplimiento diferida: alrededor del 22 de octubre de 2026 para ciertos envíos sujetos a requisitos de datos de agencias o a ciertos aranceles, lo que da un margen operativo para adaptarse en esos casos. Cuarto, el horizonte legal: independientemente de estas reglas, el de minimis legal se elimina de forma permanente el 1 de julio de 2027 bajo la ley conocida como OBBBA, así que la dirección es clara aunque los detalles administrativos sigan ajustándose. La conclusión práctica es que el envío directo al consumidor estadounidense sin impuestos, que dependía del de minimis, ya no es viable como modelo, y que las fechas exactas deben confirmarse en el Federal Register y la CBP antes de operar. BringGo Ship sigue estas reglas de cerca para orientar a los vendedores mexicanos.

¿Cómo adaptas tu modelo de venta a EE.UU.?

Deja de depender del de minimis: consolida envíos y usa entrada formal o informal con un despacho correcto, o mantén inventario en un almacén de EE.UU. para entregar localmente. Calcula los aranceles en tu precio y verifica el estado vigente de las reglas, porque el tema sigue en movimiento.

Adaptarse al fin del de minimis es sobre todo un cambio de modelo, y hay caminos claros según cómo vendas. El primero es dejar de enviar paquetes sueltos que dependían de la exención y, en su lugar, consolidar: reunir varios pedidos o traer inventario en volumen, y hacer la entrada formal o informal correcta con un despacho bien hecho, de modo que los aranceles se paguen una vez y de forma ordenada en lugar de detener paquetes en la frontera. El segundo, para quien vende al consumidor estadounidense de forma recurrente, es mantener inventario dentro de Estados Unidos, en un almacén, y entregar localmente; así el cruce se hace una vez sobre el lote y el cliente recibe rápido, sin que cada paquete individual enfrente el nuevo régimen. El tercero, transversal a todo, es incorporar los aranceles aplicables a tu precio, porque el costo que antes no existía por la exención ahora es real y debe estar en tu margen, no ser una sorpresa. Y el cuarto, indispensable, es verificar el estado vigente de las reglas antes de cada operación, porque como vimos hay plazos de comentarios, fechas diferidas y un horizonte de eliminación permanente en 2027, y el detalle administrativo puede cambiar. El mensaje central es que el modelo de envío directo sin impuestos terminó, y que consolidar, despachar bien y, cuando aplique, almacenar en EE.UU. son las respuestas sólidas. BringGo Ship opera el corredor en ambas direcciones y ayuda a estructurar estos modelos conforme a las reglas de 2026.

De minimis de EE.UU.: antes y en 2026

AspectoAntesEn 2026
Exención de 800 USDVigente (Section 321)Suspendida indefinidamente
Regla no postalAplicabaRige desde 24 jun 2026
Regla postalAplicabaRige desde 24 jul 2026
Envío de bajo valorSin impuestosEntrada formal/informal + aranceles
Horizonte legalEstableEliminación permanente 1 jul 2027

Definiciones

  • De minimis (Section 321): El de minimis de la Section 321 era la exención que permitía entrar a EE.UU. paquetes de hasta 800 dólares sin pagar impuestos, ahora suspendida.
  • Entrada formal o informal: Es el proceso aduanero que ahora requieren los envíos de bajo valor a EE.UU. para declararse y pagar los aranceles aplicables.
  • OBBBA: La ley OBBBA elimina de forma permanente el de minimis legal a partir del 1 de julio de 2027.

Preguntas frecuentes

¿Sigue vigente el de minimis de EE.UU. para envíos desde México?

No como antes. En 2026 EE.UU. suspendió de forma indefinida el de minimis de 800 dólares, así que los paquetes de bajo valor ya no entran libres de impuestos. Ahora requieren entrada formal o informal y pagan los aranceles aplicables. Es un tema en movimiento, así que verifica el estado vigente antes de enviar.

¿Desde cuándo aplica el cambio?

La CBP publicó las reglas el 24 de junio de 2026. La regla para todos los modos excepto el postal entró en vigor el 24 de junio, y la regla postal entra en vigor el 24 de julio de 2026. El plazo de comentarios cierra el 24 de julio y hay una fecha de cumplimiento diferida cerca del 22 de octubre de 2026 para ciertos envíos.

¿Qué pasa con los paquetes de bajo valor ahora?

Ya no entran libres de impuestos. Requieren una entrada formal o informal ante la aduana y pagan los aranceles que correspondan. El modelo de enviar paquetes sueltos al consumidor estadounidense sin impuestos, que dependía del de minimis, ya no es viable como antes.

¿Cómo adapto mi venta al consumidor de EE.UU.?

Deja de depender del de minimis: consolida envíos y usa entrada formal o informal con un despacho correcto, o mantén inventario en un almacén de EE.UU. para entregar localmente. Incorpora los aranceles a tu precio y verifica el estado vigente de las reglas, porque el tema sigue en movimiento.

¿Esto es permanente?

La suspensión de 2026 es indefinida, y además el de minimis legal se elimina de forma permanente el 1 de julio de 2027 bajo la ley OBBBA. Así que la dirección es clara. Aun así, los detalles administrativos, plazos y fechas diferidas siguen ajustándose, por lo que conviene confirmar el estado vigente en la CBP y el Federal Register.

Adapta tu venta a EE.UU. con el corredor de BringGo Ship

Fuentes

  • Federal Register (CBP interim final rules) (federalregister.gov)
  • US Customs and Border Protection (cbp.gov)
  • USTR (ustr.gov)

Nota: Este contenido es solo informativo y no constituye asesoría legal, fiscal ni aduanera. Las tarifas y reglas pueden cambiar con frecuencia en 2026; verifica los datos vigentes con una fuente oficial (SAT, DOF, CBP) o con nuestro agente aduanal antes de actuar.

translations.title: Mercado Libre Vs Amazon México: Comparativa Logística 2026
translations.excerpt: Mercado Libre y Amazon dominan el e-commerce mexicano, pero su logística no es igual. Comparamos Envíos Full y FBA para decidir cuál conviene primero en 2026.
translations.content:
Mercado Libre es el marketplace más grande de México y Amazon el segundo, y ambos premian la entrega rápida, así que en los dos conviene tener stock dentro del país. En logística se parecen: Envíos Full de Mercado Libre y FBA de Amazon son casi equivalentes, ambos guardan tu inventario y entregan por ti. Cuál empezar primero depende de tu categoría y tu público, no de la logística, que en ambos exige lo mismo: stock local.

  • Mercado Libre es el marketplace más grande de México, por delante de Amazon, en un mercado proyectado alrededor de 62 mil millones de dólares para 2026 (estimación de firmas de mercado; AMVO reporta cerca de 51 a 55 mil millones para 2025).
  • Envíos Full de Mercado Libre y Fulfillment by Amazon (FBA) son programas equivalentes: guardan tu inventario y hacen la entrega, y ambos exigen stock dentro de México.
  • Los dos marketplaces premian la entrega rápida en su algoritmo, así que el stock local decide más que el marketplace elegido.
  • El IVA de importación es 16 por ciento nacional; la mercancía de origen estadounidense entra sin arancel bajo el T-MEC con certificado de origen (SAT, USTR).
  • Un TIR documentado llega de Laredo a Monterrey en 1 a 2 días hábiles, lo que permite alimentar Full o FBA con rapidez (dato operativo).

¿Mercado Libre o Amazon México: cuál conviene primero?

Depende de tu categoría y tu público, no de la logística. En logística son casi iguales: ambos premian la entrega rápida y ambos ofrecen un programa de fulfillment (Envíos Full y FBA) que exige stock dentro de México. Empieza por donde esté tu comprador; el requisito logístico, tener inventario local, es el mismo.

La pregunta de cuál marketplace conviene primero suele plantearse como una decisión logística, pero en realidad la logística es lo que ambos tienen en común, y la diferencia está en el mercado. Mercado Libre es el marketplace más grande de México, por delante de Amazon, en un mercado proyectado alrededor de 62 mil millones de dólares para 2026, con una base de compradores muy local y su propio sistema de pagos, Mercado Pago. Amazon es el segundo, con una base fiel a Prime y fuerte en ciertas categorías. En cuanto a logística, se parecen mucho más de lo que se cree: los dos premian la entrega rápida en su algoritmo, y los dos ofrecen un programa de fulfillment casi equivalente, Envíos Full en Mercado Libre y FBA en Amazon, que guarda tu inventario y hace la entrega por ti. La consecuencia es clara: en ambos casos necesitas stock dentro de México para competir, porque enviar cada pedido desde fuera es lento y te penaliza en los dos. Entonces, cuál empezar primero no lo decide la logística sino tu producto y tu público: si tu categoría es fuerte y muy local, Mercado Libre suele dar más alcance; si vendes categorías donde Amazon domina o ya operas en Amazon en otros países, empezar ahí puede ser natural. Muchos vendedores terminan en ambos, alimentando Envíos Full y FBA desde el mismo inventario local. BringGo Ship importa y almacena tu stock dentro de México, listo para alimentar cualquiera de los dos.

¿En qué se diferencian Envíos Full y FBA?

En los detalles, no en el concepto. Ambos guardan tu inventario, hacen picking, empaque y entrega, y te dan mejor posición y velocidad. Las diferencias están en las tarifas, las reglas de preparación y el ecosistema de cada marketplace, pero el requisito de fondo, stock dentro de México, es idéntico.

Envíos Full y FBA son, en esencia, el mismo modelo aplicado a dos marketplaces, y entender sus diferencias ayuda a operar en ambos sin duplicar esfuerzo. Envíos Full es el brazo de fulfillment con almacén de Mercado Libre: tu inventario vive en sus centros, ellos hacen picking, empaque y entrega, y a cambio obtienes entregas más rápidas y una mejor posición en las búsquedas. FBA es exactamente el equivalente en Amazon: guarda tu inventario, entrega y da la insignia Prime que sube la conversión. Las diferencias reales están en los detalles operativos, no en el concepto: cada uno tiene su propia estructura de tarifas de almacenamiento y por pedido, sus propias reglas de preparación y etiquetado, por ejemplo el FNSKU en FBA, y su propio ecosistema de publicidad y reputación. Pero el requisito de fondo es idéntico en los dos: necesitas stock dentro de México, porque ambos programas guardan y entregan desde el país, y ninguno se alimenta bien enviando paquetes sueltos desde el extranjero. Esa es la buena noticia para quien vende en ambos: importas tu inventario una sola vez a México y desde ese stock local alimentas tanto Envíos Full como FBA, según lo que se venda en cada canal. Lo que necesitas resolver primero, entonces, no es cuál programa usar, sino cómo tener inventario en México de forma constante. BringGo Ship importa, despacha y almacena tu stock, y lo repone desde la frontera para que ambos programas nunca se queden sin producto.

¿Cómo alimentas ambos marketplaces desde un solo inventario?

Importando en volumen a un almacén dentro de México, despachando una vez con agente aduanal, y repartiendo ese stock a Envíos Full, FBA o entrega directa según la demanda de cada canal. La reposición rápida desde la frontera mantiene los dos abastecidos sin que tengas que importar por separado para cada uno.

La forma eficiente de vender en Mercado Libre y Amazon a la vez es tratar el inventario como uno solo y repartirlo, no importar por separado para cada canal. El flujo es este. Primero, importas tu stock en volumen a un almacén dentro de México y lo despachas una sola vez con un agente aduanal, pagando el 16 por ciento de IVA y, si la mercancía es de origen estadounidense con certificado, entrando sin arancel bajo el T-MEC. Pagar el costo de importación una vez sobre el lote baja el costo por unidad frente a enviar paquete por paquete. Segundo, desde ese inventario local repartes: mandas una parte a Envíos Full, otra a FBA, y puedes reservar una parte para entrega directa o para el canal que más venda, ajustando según la demanda real de cada uno. Tercero, repones: cuando un canal baja de stock, envías más desde ese inventario o desde la frontera, donde un TIR documentado llega a Monterrey en 1 a 2 días hábiles, así ninguno se queda sin producto en un momento de venta alta. La ventaja de este modelo es que no duplicas la importación ni el despacho para cada marketplace; importas una vez y alimentas a los dos, moviendo el stock hacia donde se vende. Esto te deja competir en ambos con la entrega rápida que los dos premian, sin el costo y la complejidad de operar dos cadenas de importación. BringGo Ship opera justamente este modelo: importa y almacena tu inventario en México y lo reparte a Envíos Full, FBA o entrega directa según lo necesites.

Mercado Libre vs Amazon México: logística (2026)

FactorMercado LibreAmazon México
Posición en el mercadoEl más grande de MéxicoSegundo, fuerte con Prime
Programa de fulfillmentEnvíos FullFBA
Requiere stock localSíSí
Premia entrega rápidaSí, en el algoritmoSí, insignia Prime
Empezar primeroAlcance local amplioCategorías Amazon / ya en Amazon

Definiciones

  • Envíos Full: Envíos Full es el programa de fulfillment con almacén de Mercado Libre, equivalente a FBA, que guarda tu inventario y hace la entrega.
  • FBA: FBA (Fulfillment by Amazon) guarda tu inventario en México, hace la entrega y otorga la insignia Prime; requiere stock dentro del país.
  • Stock local: El stock local es inventario importado en volumen y almacenado dentro de México, requisito para competir en ambos marketplaces.

Preguntas frecuentes

¿Mercado Libre o Amazon México, cuál conviene primero?

Depende de tu categoría y tu público, no de la logística. En logística son casi iguales: ambos premian la entrega rápida y ambos ofrecen un programa de fulfillment (Envíos Full y FBA) que exige stock dentro de México. Empieza por donde esté tu comprador; el requisito logístico, tener inventario local, es el mismo en los dos.

¿En qué se diferencian Envíos Full y FBA?

En los detalles operativos, no en el concepto. Ambos guardan tu inventario, hacen picking, empaque y entrega, y dan mejor posición y velocidad. Las diferencias están en las tarifas, las reglas de preparación (como el FNSKU en FBA) y el ecosistema de cada marketplace, pero el requisito de fondo, stock dentro de México, es idéntico.

¿Puedo vender en los dos con un solo inventario?

Sí, y es lo eficiente. Importas tu stock en volumen a un almacén en México, lo despachas una vez, y desde ese inventario local repartes a Envíos Full, FBA o entrega directa según la demanda de cada canal. No duplicas la importación ni el despacho para cada marketplace.

¿Necesito stock en México para los dos?

Sí. Los dos programas, Envíos Full y FBA, guardan y entregan desde México, y ambos algoritmos premian la entrega rápida. Enviar paquetes sueltos desde el extranjero es lento y te penaliza en los dos, así que el stock local es el requisito común para competir en cualquiera de ellos.

¿Cuánto cuesta importar el inventario a México?

Pagas el 16 por ciento de IVA sobre el valor en aduana, y la mercancía de origen estadounidense con certificado de origen suele entrar sin arancel bajo el T-MEC. Importar en volumen paga ese costo una vez sobre el lote, lo que baja el costo por unidad frente a enviar paquete por paquete a cada marketplace.

Alimenta Envíos Full y FBA desde un solo inventario con BringGo Ship

Fuentes

  • Mercado Libre (oficial) (mercadolibre.com.mx)
  • Amazon Seller Central (sellercentral.amazon.com.mx)
  • AMVO (e-commerce México) (amvo.org.mx)

translations.title: Calendario Comercial De México: Hot Sale, Buen Fin Y Las Fechas Clave
translations.excerpt: Las ventas en México se concentran en unas pocas fechas: Hot Sale en mayo y Buen Fin en noviembre. Aquí el calendario comercial 2026 y cómo preparar inventario y promociones a tiempo.
translations.content:
El calendario comercial de México se concentra en dos grandes eventos: Hot Sale, del 25 de mayo al 2 de junio en 2026, y Buen Fin, del 13 al 16 de noviembre en 2026, más el Día de las Madres el 10 de mayo y el pico navideño de diciembre. Para un vendedor, lo que decide las ventas no son las fechas del evento sino las semanas previas, cuando el inventario, las promociones y la logística deben estar listos.

  • Hot Sale 2026 es del 25 de mayo al 2 de junio, nueve días, organizado por la AMVO, la asociación de venta online de México (AMVO).
  • Buen Fin 2026 es del 13 al 16 de noviembre, alineado al fin de semana largo del 16 de noviembre, el mayor evento de descuentos del país (Hacienda, Concanaco, Profeco).
  • El Día de las Madres, 10 de mayo, es uno de los días de mayor gasto del año en México y antecede a Hot Sale.
  • El periodo navideño de diciembre extiende el impulso de Buen Fin hasta fin de año.
  • El inventario debe estar en tu almacén antes de cada evento; un TIR documentado llega de Laredo a Monterrey en 1 a 2 días hábiles, pero importar en volumen requiere tiempo de anticipación.

¿Qué fechas mueven las ventas en México?

Dos eventos dominan: Hot Sale, una semana de venta online a finales de mayo, del 25 de mayo al 2 de junio en 2026, y Buen Fin, un fin de semana de descuentos en noviembre, del 13 al 16 de noviembre en 2026. El Día de las Madres el 10 de mayo y las fiestas de diciembre suman dos olas más. Ese es el corazón del calendario comercial mexicano.

El consumo en México es estacional, y para un vendedor conocer las fechas que concentran la demanda es la base para planear el año. Los dos eventos más grandes son Hot Sale y Buen Fin. Hot Sale es la mayor campaña de venta en línea de México, organizada por la AMVO, la asociación mexicana de venta online, y en 2026 va del 25 de mayo al 2 de junio, nueve días, con cientos de comercios ofreciendo descuentos en electrónica, moda, hogar y viajes. Al ser un evento principalmente digital, es especialmente importante para el e-commerce. Buen Fin es el mayor evento de descuentos del país en general, un fin de semana largo anclado al feriado del 16 de noviembre, y en 2026 va del 13 al 16 de noviembre. Abarca comercio en línea y físico, y lo regulan juntos Hacienda, Concanaco y Profeco. Alrededor de estos dos, otras dos olas importan: el Día de las Madres, el 10 de mayo, uno de los días de mayor gasto del año, que construye impulso hacia Hot Sale, y el periodo navideño de diciembre, que extiende el impulso de Buen Fin hasta fin de año. Hay momentos menores, como el Día de Reyes a inicios de enero o el regreso a clases en verano, pero Hot Sale y Buen Fin son los dos que reconfiguran la demanda de un vendedor. Conocer las fechas es el primer paso; el segundo, el que de verdad decide si capturas el pico, es tener inventario y promociones listos a tiempo. BringGo Ship ayuda a que el inventario esté en México antes de cada evento.

¿Cómo preparas inventario y promociones a tiempo?

Trabajando hacia atrás desde la fecha. El inventario debe estar despachado y en tu almacén semanas antes de Hot Sale a finales de mayo y de Buen Fin a mediados de noviembre, y las promociones y campañas listas para arrancar el primer día. El stock que aún cruza la frontera durante el evento llega tarde al pico.

Lo que de verdad decide los resultados de un vendedor no son las fechas de Hot Sale y Buen Fin en sí, sino las semanas previas, porque el inventario que llega durante el evento llega tarde. La demanda se concentra en pocos días, así que si tu stock no está ya en México y listo para entregar cuando el evento abre, no puedes capturar esa demanda por muy buena que sea tu oferta. Esto obliga a planear hacia atrás desde la fecha. Para Buen Fin a mediados de noviembre, la regla práctica es despachar desde Estados Unidos a inicios de octubre, para que la mercancía cruce, se despache en aduana y llegue a tu almacén con margen antes del evento, y antes de la congestión de frontera de fin de año. Para Hot Sale a finales de mayo, la misma lógica una temporada antes: ten el inventario en el país a inicios de mayo, lo que además te coloca para la ola del Día de las Madres el 10 de mayo. En paralelo al inventario, prepara la parte comercial: las promociones, los precios, las campañas de publicidad y las fichas de producto deben estar listas para arrancar el primer día del evento, no armarse sobre la marcha. La buena noticia es que, una vez en la frontera, el cruce es rápido, un TIR documentado llega a Monterrey en 1 a 2 días hábiles, pero la importación en volumen, la consolidación, el despacho y el etiquetado requieren anticipación. La disciplina es tratar la fecha del evento como la fecha límite para estar en vivo, y trabajar hacia atrás. BringGo Ship ayuda a planear ese calendario inverso y a tener el inventario despachado y en el país antes de cada pico.

¿Cómo planeas el año comercial completo?

Con dos fechas límite de inventario: stock en el país a inicios de mayo para Hot Sale y el Día de las Madres, y a mediados de octubre para Buen Fin y diciembre. Pronostica tus productos estrella, impórtalos en volumen antes de cada uno, y ten un canal de reposición rápido desde la frontera para no quedarte sin stock durante el evento.

Planear el año comercial completo convierte la estacionalidad de una amenaza en una ventaja, y se reduce a unas pocas fechas límite y hábitos. Primero, marca las dos fechas límite de inventario, no solo las del evento: tu stock debe estar despachado y en el país a inicios de mayo para la ola de Hot Sale y el Día de las Madres, y a mediados de octubre para Buen Fin y las fiestas de diciembre. Trabajar hacia esas fechas, y no hacia la del evento, es lo que evita llegar tarde. Segundo, pronostica tus productos estrella con los datos del año anterior y las tendencias, para decidir qué importar en volumen antes de cada evento, ya que ahí es donde estar en stock y ser rápido pesa más. Tercero, importa con anticipación, despachando una vez con agente aduanal, pagando el 16 por ciento de IVA y entrando sin arancel bajo el T-MEC la mercancía de origen estadounidense con certificado. Cuarto, ten un canal de reposición rápido desde la frontera, porque el mejor problema durante Hot Sale o Buen Fin es agotarte, y un TIR que llega a Monterrey en 1 a 2 días hábiles te permite reabastecer a mitad del evento. Quinto, fija expectativas de entrega honestas para lo que vendas sin stock durante el pico, porque los tiempos se estiran cuando el volumen sube. Como las fechas exactas cambian un poco cada año, confírmalas anualmente, pero la disciplina de anticipar se mantiene. BringGo Ship acompaña este ciclo, de la importación previa al evento a la reposición a mitad de campaña en el corredor Laredo-Monterrey.

Calendario comercial de México 2026

EventoFechas 2026Inventario listo para
Día de las Madres10 de mayoInicios de mayo
Hot Sale (AMVO)25 de mayo a 2 de junioInicios de mayo
Buen Fin13 a 16 de noviembreMediados de octubre
Fiestas de diciembreHasta fin de añoMediados de octubre
Día de Reyes6 de eneroFines de diciembre

Definiciones

  • Hot Sale: Hot Sale es la mayor campaña de venta en línea de México, organizada por la AMVO, del 25 de mayo al 2 de junio en 2026.
  • Buen Fin: Buen Fin es el mayor evento de descuentos de México, un fin de semana largo de noviembre, del 13 al 16 de noviembre en 2026.
  • Fecha límite de inventario: La fecha límite de inventario es cuándo el stock debe estar en el país para vender en un evento, semanas antes del evento mismo.

Preguntas frecuentes

¿Qué fechas mueven las ventas en México?

Dos eventos dominan: Hot Sale, una semana de venta online a finales de mayo, del 25 de mayo al 2 de junio en 2026, y Buen Fin, un fin de semana de descuentos en noviembre, del 13 al 16 de noviembre en 2026. El Día de las Madres el 10 de mayo y las fiestas de diciembre suman dos olas más al calendario comercial.

¿Cuándo es Hot Sale 2026?

Hot Sale 2026 va del 25 de mayo al 2 de junio, nueve días, organizado por la AMVO, la asociación mexicana de venta online. Es un evento principalmente digital, lo que lo hace especialmente importante para el e-commerce, con cientos de comercios ofreciendo descuentos en electrónica, moda, hogar y viajes.

¿Cuándo es Buen Fin 2026?

Buen Fin 2026 va del 13 al 16 de noviembre, un fin de semana largo anclado al feriado del 16 de noviembre. Es el mayor evento de descuentos de México, abarca comercio en línea y físico, y lo regulan juntos Hacienda, Concanaco y Profeco.

¿Cuándo debo tener el inventario listo para estos eventos?

Antes del evento, no durante. Planea hacia atrás: ten el inventario despachado y en el país a inicios de mayo para Hot Sale y el Día de las Madres, y a mediados de octubre para Buen Fin y diciembre. El stock que aún cruza la frontera durante el evento llega tarde al pico, ya que la demanda se concentra en pocos días.

¿Cambian las fechas cada año?

Un poco, sí. Buen Fin se ancla al fin de semana del 16 de noviembre y Hot Sale a finales de mayo, pero las fechas exactas las fijan cada año la AMVO y, para Buen Fin, Hacienda, Concanaco y Profeco. Confirma las fechas oficiales anualmente; la disciplina de anticipar el inventario se mantiene igual.

translations.title: Dropshipping'de Teslimat Süresi: Müşteriye Ne Söz Vermeli?
translations.excerpt: Dropshipping'de teslimat süresi doğrudan itibarındır. Modeline göre ne söz vereceğini bilmek, iyi yorumla iade arasındaki farktır. İşte Meksika için gerçekçi süreler.
translations.content:
Meksika'ya dropshipping'de gerçekçi teslimat sözü modeline bağlıdır: her siparişi yurt dışından tek tek gönderiyorsan dürüst süre bir haftadan fazladır, çünkü her paket ayrı gümrük görür. Meksika içinde stoğun varsa 1-2 günde yerel teslim edersin. Kural şudur: tutabileceğin süreyi söz ver, iyimser olanı değil; çünkü Meksikalı alıcı hızlı teslimatı bekler ve tutulmayan söz iade, itiraz ve kötü yorum doğurur.

  • Sınır ötesi dropshipping'de her siparişi yurt dışından göndermek, paket başına gümrük demektir ve teslimat bir hafta veya daha uzun sürer.
  • Meksika içinde stokla teslimat yereldir ve şehir merkezlerinde genellikle 1-2 gündür.
  • İyi belgelenmiş bir TIR, Laredo'dan Monterrey'e 1-2 iş gününde ulaşır; bu, yerel stoğu hızlı beslemeyi sağlar (operasyon verisi).
  • Tutamayacağın bir süre söz vermek iade, itiraz ve kötü yorum doğurur.
  • ABD menşeli mal menşe belgesiyle T-MEC kapsamında genellikle gümrüksüz girer; ithalatta IVA yüzde 16'dır (SAT, USTR).

Dropshipping'de hangi teslimat sözü gerçekçi?

Modeline bağlı. Her siparişi yurt dışından gönderiyorsan dürüst söz bir haftadan fazladır, çünkü her paket ayrı gümrük görür. Meksika içinde stoğun varsa 1-2 gün yerel teslim söz verebilirsin. Anahtar, operasyonunun gerçek süresini söz vermek; tutamayacağın iyimser bir süreyi değil.

Dropshipping'de teslimat süresi bir lojistik detayı değil, itibarındır; ve Mercado Libre ile Amazon'un hızı standart yaptığı Meksika'da alıcı karşılaştırır. O yüzden ilk kural tutabileceğin süreyi söz vermektir, bunun için de gerçek modelini bilmen gerekir. İki çok farklı senaryo var. Birincisinde her siparişi, satıldığı anda yurt dışından, tipik olarak ABD veya Asya'dan gönderirsin. Burada her paket ayrı bir ithalat olarak tek tek gümrük görür, ve dürüst teslimat süresi uluslararası taşıma artı gümrük ile bir haftanın büyük kısmı veya daha fazladır. Bu modelle iki üç gün söz vermek, kendini başarısızlığa hazırlamaktır. İkinci senaryoda Meksika içinde stoğun vardır, toplu ithal edilmiş ve yurt içi sevk edilir; burada teslimat yereldir ve şehir merkezlerinde 1-2 gün söz verebilirsin, ki bu Meksikalı alıcının beklediği standarttır. İkisi arasındaki fark nüans değil, ne söz verebileceğini belirler. Yapabileceğin en kötü şey, birinci modelle çalışırken ikinci modelin süresini söz vermektir. Modelin sınır ötesiyse süre konusunda şeffaf ol; hızda yarışmak istiyorsan stoğu Meksika'ya taşı. BringGo Ship, envanterini Meksika içinde, Monterrey'de ithal edip stoklar, böylece ithalat süresi yerine hızlı yerel teslimat söz verebilirsin.

Sınır ötesi dropshipping neden yavaş, nasıl anlatılır?

Yavaştır çünkü her sipariş sınırı tek tek geçer: paket başına uluslararası taşıma artı gümrük. Şeffaf anlat: gerçekçi bir aralık ver, ürünün yurt dışından geldiğini açıkla, süreyi küçük punto arasına saklama. Bilgilendirilmiş müşteri, sürprize uğrayandan çok daha az kızar.

Sınır ötesi dropshipping'in neden yavaş olduğunu anlamak, onu saklamak yerine iyi anlatmana yardım eder. Stok tutmadan, her siparişi yurt dışından gönderdiğinde, her satış tek tek uluslararası bir sevkiyat tetikler: paket menşe ülkeden yola çıkar, sınıra ulaşır, kendi başına bir işlem olarak gümrükten geçer ve sonra teslim edilir. Bu süreç doğası gereği bir haftanın büyük kısmını veya daha fazlasını alır, ve darboğaz son mil değil, paket başına gümrük geçişi olduğu için modeli değiştirmeden fazla kısaltılamaz. İnsanın içinden bu süreyi saklamak gelir, ama iade, itiraz ve kötü yorumu tetikleyen tam da budur: müşteri beklemekten çok, aldatılmış hissetmekten kızar. Doğru anlatımın üç parçası var. Birincisi, gerçekçi ve biraz temkinli bir aralık ver, örneğin şu kadar iş günü, ve onu tut veya aş. İkincisi, ürünün yurt dışından geldiğini doğal bir dille açıkla; birçok müşteri fiyat veya özellik bunu haklı çıkarıyorsa kabul eder. Üçüncüsü, süreyi küçük punto arasına saklama, 'ekspres kargo' gibi tutamayacağın ifadelerle çelişme. Şeffaflık sadece anlaşmazlığı önlemez, tekrar satış için güven kurar. Ve süre en büyük dönüşüm frenin olduğunda çözüm yapısaldır: yurt dışından paket göndermeyi bırakıp yerel stoğa geçmek. BringGo Ship tam bunu sağlar: toplu ithal edip yurt içi teslim, böylece süre bir bahane olmaktan çıkar.

İthalat süresinden hızlı yerel teslimata nasıl geçilir?

Envanterini Meksika içinde bir depoya toplu ithal edip yurt içi sevk ederek. En çok satan ürünlerini toplu getirir, bir kez gümrükler, sonra her sipariş sınır geçmek yerine Meksika'dan 1-2 günde teslim edilir. Sınırdan hızlı reposition stoğu kesintisiz tutar.

İthalat süresinden hızlı yerel teslimata geçmek, aslında envanterinin nerede yaşadığını değiştirmektir ve göründüğünden daha ulaşılabilir. Model şöyle işler: her siparişi yurt dışından göndermek yerine, en çok satan ürünlerini Meksika içindeki bir depoya toplu ithal edersin, gümrük bedelini tüm lot üzerinden bir kez ödersin, ve o andan sonra her sipariş Meksika'dan yurt içi olarak 1-2 günde teslim edilir. Bu, ne söz verebileceğini tümüyle değiştirir, çünkü artık bir sınır geçişine değil, ülkede hazır bekleyen stoğa karşı satarsın. Reposition lojistiği modeli ayakta tutar: yerel stok azaldığında sınırdan bir tamamlama gönderirsin, ve iyi belgelenmiş bir TIR Laredo'dan Monterrey'e 1-2 iş gününde ulaştığı için hızlı ve kesintisiz tamamlarsın. Ek avantajlar da var: ithalat bedeli lot üzerinden bir kez ödendiği için sipariş başına maliyet düşer; ve malın ABD menşeli ve belgeliyse T-MEC kapsamında genellikle gümrüksüz girerek marjı korur. Denge noktası basittir: bir ürün istikrarlı satıyorsa, onu yerel stokta tutmak hem sürede hem sipariş başına maliyette neredeyse her zaman yurt dışından paket göndermeyi yener. Pratik strateji, sınır ötesiyle başlayıp ne sattığını görmek, sonra kanıtlanan ürünleri yerel stoğa taşımaktır. BringGo Ship bu geçişi işletir: envanterini ithal eder, gümrükler ve Meksika içinde stoklar, böylece yerel teslim edersin.

Meksika'ya dropshipping: modele göre süre

ModelTeslimat süresiNot
Yurt dışından paket paketBir hafta veya daha fazlaPaket başına gümrük
Meksika içinde stok1-2 gün (şehir içi)Yurt içi teslim
Sınırdan repositionLaredo-Monterrey 1-2 günStoğu kesintisiz tutar
Ne söz vermeliGerçek süreyi, ideali değilŞeffaflık iadeyi önler

Tanımlar

  • Sınır ötesi dropshipping: Sınır ötesi dropshipping, her siparişi satıldığında yurt dışından gönderir; paket başına gümrük geçişi teslimat süresini uzatır.
  • Yerel stok: Yerel stok, toplu ithal edilip Meksika içinde depolanan envanterdir ve 1-2 günde yurt içi teslimatı sağlar.
  • Reposition: Reposition, yerel stoğu sınırdan gelen sevkiyatlarla tamamlamaktır; Monterrey'e 1-2 iş gününde ulaşır.

Sık sorulan sorular

Dropshipping'de hangi teslimat sözü gerçekçi?

Modeline bağlı. Her siparişi yurt dışından gönderiyorsan dürüst söz bir haftadan fazladır, çünkü her paket ayrı gümrük görür. Meksika içinde stoğun varsa 1-2 gün yerel teslim söz verebilirsin. Her zaman operasyonunun gerçek süresini söz ver, tutamayacağın iyimser bir süreyi değil.

Sınır ötesi dropshipping neden yavaş?

Çünkü her sipariş yurt dışından tek tek gönderilir: uluslararası taşınır ve paket paket gümrükten geçer. Bu süreç bir haftanın büyük kısmını veya daha fazlasını alır ve modeli değiştirmeden fazla kısaltılamaz, çünkü darboğaz son mil değil, paket başına sınır geçişidir.

Uzun teslimat süresini satış kaybetmeden nasıl anlatırım?

Şeffaflıkla: gerçekçi ve biraz temkinli bir aralık ver, ürünün yurt dışından geldiğini açıkla, süreyi küçük punto arasına saklama. Bilgilendirilmiş müşteri sürprize uğrayandan çok daha az kızar. Şeffaflık iade ve itirazı önler, tekrar satış için güven kurar.

Meksika'da nasıl daha hızlı teslim ederim?

Envanterini Meksika içinde bir depoya toplu ithal edip yurt içi sevk ederek. En çok satanları toplu getirir, bir kez gümrükler, ve her sipariş sınır geçmek yerine Meksika'dan 1-2 günde teslim edilir. Sınırdan reposition, Monterrey'e 1-2 gün transitle, stoğu kesintisiz tutar.

Yerel stoğa ne zaman geçmeliyim?

Bir ürün istikrarlı satmaya başladığında. O noktada, onu Meksika içinde stokta tutmak hem teslimat süresinde hem sipariş başına maliyette neredeyse her zaman yurt dışından paket göndermeyi yener, çünkü gümrük lot üzerinden bir kez ödenir. Strateji, sınır ötesiyle doğrulayıp kanıtlananı yerele taşımaktır.
