translations.title: Meksika'da En Çok Satan Türk Ürünleri: Kategori Fırsatları
translations.excerpt: Türkiye'nin güçlü olduğu kategoriler Meksika'da talep gören kategorilerle örtüşüyor. Ev tekstilinden gıdaya, hangi Türk ürünlerinin fırsat taşıdığını kategori kategori inceliyoruz.
translations.content:
Meksika'da fırsat taşıyan Türk ürünleri, Türkiye'nin üretimde güçlü olduğu kategorilerle Meksika'nın talebinin kesiştiği yerlerdedir: ev tekstili (havlu, bornoz, nevresim), giyim, işlenmiş gıda ve şekerleme, kozmetik ve kişisel bakım, ve ev/mutfak ürünleri. Bir kategoriyi seçerken tek başına 'çok satar' demek yeterli değil; Türk kalitesinin farkı, doğru fiyat konumu ve hızlı yerel teslimat birleştiğinde satar.

  • Türkiye ev tekstilinde (havlu, bornoz, nevresim) küresel olarak güçlü bir üretici; bu kategori Meksika'da istikrarlı talep görür.
  • Giyim, işlenmiş gıda/şekerleme, kozmetik ve ev/mutfak ürünleri Türkiye'nin ihracatta güçlü olduğu diğer kategorilerdir.
  • Bir ürünün Meksika'da satması için kalite farkı, doğru fiyat konumu ve hızlı yerel teslimatın birleşmesi gerekir.
  • Çoğu perakende ürün Meksika'ya girişte İspanyolca NOM etiketi gerektirir; gıda ve kozmetik ek sağlık/COFEPRIS izinleri isteyebilir.
  • Yerel stokla teslimat 1-2 gündür ve Meksikalı alıcının beklediği standarttır; ABD menşeli olmayan Türk malı T-MEC gümrüksüzlüğünden yararlanmaz, gümrük vergisi hesaba katılmalıdır (SAT, USTR).

Hangi Türk ürünleri Meksika'da satar?

Türkiye'nin üretimde güçlü olduğu kategorilerle Meksika'nın talebinin kesiştiği ürünler: ev tekstili (havlu, bornoz, nevresim), giyim, işlenmiş gıda ve şekerleme, kozmetik ve kişisel bakım, ev ve mutfak ürünleri. Fırsat, kategorinin büyüklüğünde değil, Türk kalitesinin farkını doğru fiyatla ve hızlı teslimatla buluşturmakta.

Meksika'da hangi Türk ürünlerinin satacağını sormanın doğru yolu, iki listenin kesişimine bakmaktır: Türkiye'nin üretimde gerçekten güçlü olduğu kategoriler ve Meksika'nın istikrarlı talep gösterdiği kategoriler. Bu kesişim birkaç net alan verir. Ev tekstili başta gelir: Türkiye havlu, bornoz ve nevresimde küresel olarak tanınan bir üretici, ve bu ürünler Meksika'da hem tüketici hem otel/konaklama tarafında istikrarlı talep görür; kalite farkı hikayesi burada güçlüdür. Giyim ikinci alandır; Türk konfeksiyonu kalite-fiyat dengesiyle bilinir. İşlenmiş gıda ve şekerleme üçüncüsüdür; Türk lokumu, kuruyemiş, baklava benzeri ürünler ve bazı bakliyat/gıda ürünleri niş ama sadık talep bulabilir, ancak bunlar sağlık iznine tabidir. Kozmetik ve kişisel bakım dördüncü alandır; doğal içerikli ürünler ve belirli kategoriler ilgi görür. Beşincisi ev ve mutfak ürünleridir. Önemli uyarı: 'en çok satan' ifadesini kesin rakamlarla değil, kategori fırsatı olarak okumak gerekir, çünkü gerçek satış ürüne, fiyatlamaya, listelemeye ve rekabete bağlıdır. Bir kategori 'büyük' diye değil, Türk kalitesinin farkı doğru fiyat konumu ve hızlı yerel teslimatla birleştiğinde satar. BringGo Ship, hangi kategoride olursan ol, ürünü Meksika'ya ithal edip yerel stoklayarak hızlı teslimatı mümkün kılar.

Bir kategoriyi nasıl değerlendirirsin?

Üç soruyla: Türk ürününün gerçek bir kalite veya fiyat farkı var mı; Meksika'da bu kategoride talep ve makul rekabet var mı; ve uyum ile lojistik (NOM etiketi, gerekli izinler, gümrük vergisi, hızlı teslimat) marjı ayakta tutuyor mu? Üçü birden olumluysa kategori gerçek bir fırsattır.

Bir kategoriyi ciddi değerlendirmek, 'Türk malı iyidir' genellemesinin ötesine geçmeyi gerektirir, ve üç soru işi netleştirir. Birinci soru farklılaşma: bu Türk ürününün Meksika pazarındaki alternatiflere karşı gerçek bir farkı var mı? Ev tekstilinde bu fark kalite ve dokudur; giyimde kalite-fiyat dengesidir; gıdada özgünlüktür. Fark yoksa, sadece 'Türk' olmak satış nedeni değildir. İkinci soru talep ve rekabet: Meksika'da bu kategoride istikrarlı bir talep var mı, ve rekabet makul mü yoksa büyük yerel/Çin oyuncularıyla dolu mu? Amazon ve Mercado Libre'de kategoriyi hızlıca taramak bunu gösterir. Üçüncü soru uyum ve lojistik: ürün Meksika'ya girişte ne gerektiriyor ve marj ayakta kalıyor mu? Çoğu perakende ürün İspanyolca NOM etiketi ister; gıda ve kozmetik sağlık/COFEPRIS izinlerine tabi olabilir; ve kritik bir nokta, Türkiye menşeli mal T-MEC üyesi olmadığı için gümrük vergisi öder, bu da hesaba katılmalıdır. Üç soruya da olumlu cevap veren bir kategori gerçek bir fırsattır; birinde takılan kategori, cazip görünse de riskini bilmen gereken bir kategoridir. Bu değerlendirmeyi yaptıktan sonra kalan iş operasyoneldir: ürünü ithal etmek, uyumlu hale getirmek ve hızlı teslim edebilmek. BringGo Ship bu operasyonel tarafı, ithalattan NOM etiketlemeye ve yerel stoklamaya kadar üstlenir.

Fırsatı satışa nasıl çevirirsin?

Doğru kategoriyi seçtikten sonra: ürünü toplu ithal et, NOM etiketi ve gerekli izinleri hazırla, gümrük vergisini fiyata kat, ve Meksika içinde stoklayıp 1-2 günde teslim et. Küçük bir partiyle test et, satan ürünü büyüt. Hız ve uyum, kaliteli ürünü gerçek satışa çeviren şeydir.

Bir kategori fırsatını gerçek satışa çevirmek, doğru ürünü seçmekle biter değil, başlar; ve adımlar nettir. Önce küçük bir partiyle test et: bir kategorinin fırsat taşıması, senin özel ürününün satacağını garanti etmez, o yüzden küçük bir ithalatla listele, gerçek talebi ve fiyat noktasını gör. İkincisi, uyumu baştan hallet: ürünün İspanyolca NOM etiketini hazırla, gıda veya kozmetikse COFEPRIS iznini araştır, çünkü bunlar eksikse mal gümrükte durur. Üçüncüsü, gümrük vergisini fiyata kat: Türkiye menşeli mal T-MEC gümrüksüzlüğünden yararlanmadığı için ürünün tarifesine göre vergi öder, ve bu maliyet fiyatlamanda görünmeli, sonradan marjını yememeli. Dördüncüsü, hızlı teslim et: kaliteli bir ürünü bile yavaş teslimat öldürür, o yüzden satan ürünleri Meksika içinde stokla ve 1-2 günde yurt içi teslim et; bu, Meksikalı alıcının beklediği ve algoritmanın ödüllendirdiği standarttır. Beşincisi, kanıtlananı büyüt: küçük partide satan ürünü toplu ithal edip ölçekle, satmayanı bırak. Bu döngü, bir kategori fırsatını sürdürülebilir bir işe çevirir. Dikkat edilecek şey, 'Türk ürünü çok satar' varsayımına değil, seçilen ürünün gerçek verisine güvenmektir. BringGo Ship bu döngüyü besler: küçük test partisinden toplu ithalata, NOM etiketlemeye ve hızlı yerel teslimata kadar operasyonu yürütür.

Meksika'da fırsat taşıyan Türk kategori alanları

GiyimKalite-fiyat dengesiNOM etiketi

KozmetikDoğal içerikCOFEPRIS izni

Tanımlar

  • Kategori fırsatı: Kategori fırsatı, Türkiye'nin üretimde güçlü olduğu ile Meksika'nın talep gösterdiği alanların kesiştiği ürün grubudur.
  • NOM etiketi: NOM etiketi, çoğu perakende ürünün Meksika'ya girişte taşıması gereken İspanyolca zorunlu etikettir.
  • COFEPRIS izni: COFEPRIS izni, gıda ve kozmetik gibi ürünlerin Meksika'ya girişi için gereken sağlık iznidir.

Sık sorulan sorular

Hangi Türk ürünleri Meksika'da satar?

Türkiye'nin üretimde güçlü olduğu kategorilerle Meksika talebinin kesiştiği ürünler: ev tekstili (havlu, bornoz, nevresim), giyim, işlenmiş gıda ve şekerleme, kozmetik ve kişisel bakım, ev ve mutfak ürünleri. Fırsat, kategorinin büyüklüğünde değil, kaliteyi doğru fiyat ve hızlı teslimatla buluşturmakta.

Bir kategoriyi nasıl değerlendiririm?

Üç soruyla: Türk ürününün gerçek bir kalite veya fiyat farkı var mı; Meksika'da bu kategoride talep ve makul rekabet var mı; ve uyum ile lojistik (NOM etiketi, izinler, gümrük vergisi, hızlı teslimat) marjı ayakta tutuyor mu? Üçü birden olumluysa kategori gerçek bir fırsattır.

Türkiye menşeli mal Meksika'da gümrüksüz girer mi?

Hayır. T-MEC gümrüksüzlüğü ABD ve Kanada menşeli mallar içindir; Türkiye T-MEC üyesi değildir, bu yüzden Türk malı ürünün tarifesine göre gümrük vergisi öder. Bu maliyet fiyatlamaya baştan katılmalı. Menşeden bağımsız olarak ithalatta yüzde 16 IVA da ödenir.

Gıda ve kozmetik satmak için ek izin gerekir mi?

Genellikle evet. Gıda ve kozmetik gibi ürünler Meksika'ya girişte sağlık iznine, yani COFEPRIS iznine tabi olabilir, ve çoğu ürün ayrıca İspanyolca NOM etiketi ister. Bu izinler eksikse mal gümrükte durur, o yüzden kategoriyi seçmeden önce araştırmak gerekir.

Fırsatı nasıl gerçek satışa çeviririm?

Ürünü küçük bir partiyle test et, NOM etiketi ve gerekli izinleri hazırla, gümrük vergisini fiyata kat, ve Meksika içinde stoklayıp 1-2 günde teslim et. Küçük partide satanı toplu ithal edip büyüt, satmayanı bırak. Hız ve uyum, kaliteli ürünü sürdürülebilir satışa çevirir.

Türk ürününü Meksika'da yerel stokla sat: BringGo Ship

Kaynaklar

  • SAT (Meksika vergi idaresi) (sat.gob.mx)
  • COFEPRIS (sağlık otoritesi) (gob.mx)
  • AMVO (e-ticaret Meksika) (amvo.org.mx)

Not: Bu içerik yalnızca bilgilendirme amaçlıdır ve hukuki, mali veya gümrük danışmanlığı değildir. Vergi oranları ve mevzuat 2026'da sık değişebilir; işlem yapmadan önce resmi kaynaktan (SAT, DOF, CBP) veya lisanslı gümrük müşavirimizden güncel bilgiyi teyit edin.

translations.title: Meksika Alışveriş Takvimi: Hot Sale, Buen Fin Ve Zirve Tarihler
translations.excerpt: Meksika'da satışlar birkaç tarihte toplanır: Mayıs'ta Hot Sale, Kasım'da Buen Fin. Türk satıcı için asıl mesele, uzun tedarik süresi nedeniyle stoğu çok önceden hazır etmek.
translations.content:
Meksika'nın iki büyük satış zirvesi Hot Sale (2026'da 25 Mayıs-2 Haziran) ve Buen Fin'dir (2026'da 13-16 Kasım); ayrıca 10 Mayıs Anneler Günü ve Aralık yılbaşı dönemi. Türk satıcı için kritik nokta, tedarik zincirinin uzun olmasıdır: mal Türkiye'den yola çıkıp ABD üzerinden Meksika'ya ulaştığı için, stoğun etkinlikten haftalar önce ülkede hazır olması gerekir, aksi halde zirveyi kaçırır.

  • Hot Sale 2026 = 25 Mayıs-2 Haziran (9 gün), AMVO'nun düzenlediği Meksika'nın en büyük online satış kampanyası (AMVO).
  • Buen Fin 2026 = 13-16 Kasım, 16 Kasım Devrim Günü uzun hafta sonuna bağlı, ülkenin en büyük indirim etkinliği (Hacienda, Concanaco, Profeco).
  • 10 Mayıs Anneler Günü Meksika'nın en yüksek harcama günlerinden biridir ve Hot Sale öncesi bir dalga yaratır.
  • Türk satıcının tedarik zinciri uzundur (Türkiye → ABD → Meksika), bu yüzden stoğu diğer satıcılardan daha erken hazır etmesi gerekir.
  • Sınırdan iç sevkiyat hızlıdır: iyi belgelenmiş bir TIR Laredo'dan Monterrey'e 1-2 iş gününde ulaşır, ama toplu ithalat ve okyanus/uçak taşıması ciddi anticipasyon ister (operasyon verisi).

Meksika'da satış zirveleri hangi tarihlerde?

İki büyük etkinlik: Hot Sale, Mayıs sonunda bir online satış haftası, 2026'da 25 Mayıs-2 Haziran; ve Buen Fin, Kasım'da bir indirim hafta sonu, 2026'da 13-16 Kasım. 10 Mayıs Anneler Günü ve Aralık yılbaşı dönemi iki dalga daha ekler. Bunlar Meksika alışveriş takviminin kalbidir.

Meksika'da tüketim mevsimseldir ve satışların büyük kısmı birkaç tarihte toplanır; bir satıcı için bu tarihleri bilmek yılı planlamanın temelidir. En büyük iki etkinlik Hot Sale ve Buen Fin'dir. Hot Sale, Meksika'nın en büyük online satış kampanyasıdır; ülkenin online satış derneği AMVO tarafından düzenlenir ve 2026'da 25 Mayıs-2 Haziran arası, dokuz gün sürer; yüzlerce satıcı elektronik, moda, ev ve seyahatte indirim sunar. Ağırlıkla dijital bir etkinlik olması, e-ticaret için özellikle önemli kılar. Buen Fin, ülkenin genel olarak en büyük indirim etkinliğidir; 16 Kasım Devrim Günü tatiline bağlı uzun bir hafta sonudur ve 2026'da 13-16 Kasım arasıdır. Hem online hem fiziksel perakendeyi kapsar ve Hacienda, Concanaco ve Profeco tarafından birlikte düzenlenir. Bu ikisinin etrafında iki dalga daha önemlidir: 10 Mayıs Anneler Günü, yılın en yüksek harcama günlerinden biri, ki Hot Sale'e momentum taşır; ve Aralık yılbaşı dönemi, Buen Fin momentumunu yıl sonuna kadar uzatır. Ocak başındaki Día de Reyes veya yaz okul dönüşü gibi daha küçük anlar da vardır ama satıcının talebini asıl yeniden şekillendiren Hot Sale ve Buen Fin'dir. Tarihleri bilmek ilk adımdır; zirveyi yakalayıp yakalayamayacağını asıl belirleyen ikinci adım, stoğu zamanında hazır etmektir. BringGo Ship, Türk satıcının stoğunu etkinlikten önce Meksika'da hazır etmesine yardım eder.

Türk satıcı stoğunu ne zaman hazır etmeli?

Diğerlerinden daha erken. Çünkü mal Türkiye'den ABD üzerinden Meksika'ya uzun bir yol kat eder. Stoğun Hot Sale (Mayıs sonu) ve Buen Fin (Kasım ortası) öncesinde ülkede gümrüklenmiş ve hazır olması için, ithalatı çok önceden başlatmak gerekir. Etkinlik sırasında hâlâ yolda olan stok zirveyi kaçırır.

Bir Türk satıcı için sonuçları belirleyen, Hot Sale ve Buen Fin tarihleri değil, onlardan çok önceki haftalardır; çünkü Türkiye'den gelen malın tedarik zinciri uzundur ve geç başlanırsa mal etkinlikte yolda olur. Zincir şöyledir: mal Türkiye'den yola çıkar, tipik olarak okyanus veya uçak taşımasıyla ABD'ye gelir, Laredo gibi bir sınır noktasında konsolide edilir, gümrüklenip Meksika'ya geçer ve Monterrey deposuna ulaşır. Bu zincirin ilk halkaları, yani Türkiye'den ABD'ye taşıma, haftalar sürebilir. İyi haber, zincirin son halkasının hızlı olmasıdır: iyi belgelenmiş bir TIR Laredo'dan Monterrey'e 1-2 iş gününde ulaşır. Ama toplam süre, bu son hızlı adım değil, baştaki uzun taşımadır. Bu yüzden Türk satıcı, yerel bir Meksikalı satıcıdan daha erken planlamalıdır. Buen Fin için, Kasım ortası, malın Türkiye'den çok önce, yaz sonu veya Eylül başında yola çıkması akıllıcadır; böylece okyanus taşıması, ABD'ye varış, konsolidasyon, gümrük ve yıl sonu sınır yoğunluğu öncesi Meksika'ya ulaşma için tampon kalır. Hot Sale için, Mayıs sonu, aynı mantık bir mevsim önce: malı ilkbahar başında yola çıkar, ki bu seni 10 Mayıs Anneler Günü dalgasına da hazırlar. Kural, etkinlik tarihini 'canlı olma son tarihi' kabul edip geriye doğru, uzun tedarik süresini hesaba katarak planlamaktır. BringGo Ship, bu geriye dönük planlamada ve ABD'den Meksika'ya olan halkada stoğu zamanında hazır etmeye yardım eder.

Ticari yılı takvime göre nasıl planlarsın?

İki stok son tarihiyle: Hot Sale ve Anneler Günü için ilkbahar başında, Buen Fin ve Aralık için yaz sonu-Eylül'de mal yola çıkmış olmalı. En çok satacağın ürünleri öngör, her etkinlikten önce toplu ithal et, ve etkinlik ortasında bitmemek için sınırdan hızlı reposition yolunu hazır tut.

Ticari yılı Meksika takvimine göre planlamak, mevsimselliği bir tehditten avantaja çevirir ve birkaç son tarih ile alışkanlığa dayanır. Önce iki stok son tarihini işaretle, sadece etkinlik tarihlerini değil: uzun tedarik süren nedeniyle malın Hot Sale ve Anneler Günü için ilkbahar başında, Buen Fin ve Aralık için yaz sonu veya Eylül başında Türkiye'den yola çıkmış olması gerekir. Bu son tarihlere göre çalışmak, geç kalmayı önler. İkincisi, en çok satacağın ürünleri geçen yılın verisi ve trendlerle öngör, çünkü stokta ve hızlı olmanın en çok işe yaradığı ürünler bunlardır. Üçüncüsü, her etkinlikten önce toplu ithal et, gümrüğü bir kez yaptır, gümrük değeri üzerinden yüzde 16 IVA öde ve Türkiye menşeli mal T-MEC dışı olduğu için gümrük vergisini hesaba kat. Dördüncüsü, sınırdan hızlı reposition yolunu hazır tut: Hot Sale veya Buen Fin sırasında yaşanacak en iyi sorun stoğun bitmesidir, ve Monterrey'e 1-2 iş gününde ulaşan bir sevkiyat sana etkinlik ortasında tamamlama imkânı verir, tabii ABD tarafında yedek stoğun varsa. Beşincisi, zirve sırasında stoksuz sattığın ürünlerde dürüst teslimat süresi ver, çünkü hacim arttığında süreler uzar. Bunu yaparsan takvim senin için çalışır: her etkinliğe stoğu canlı, hızlı teslim eden ve sipariş başına maliyeti düşük halde girersin. Etkinlik tarihleri her yıl biraz kaydığı için yıllık teyit et, ama planlama disiplini aynı kalır. BringGo Ship bu döngüyü, etkinlik öncesi ithalattan etkinlik ortası reposition'a kadar destekler.

Tanımlar

  • Buen Fin: Buen Fin, Meksika'nın en büyük indirim etkinliğidir; Kasım'da uzun bir hafta sonu, 2026'da 13-16 Kasım'dır.
  • Stok son tarihi: Stok son tarihi, malın bir etkinlikte satışa canlı olması için ülkede hazır olması gereken tarihtir; Türk satıcı için uzun tedarik nedeniyle çok erkendir.

Sık sorulan sorular

Meksika'da satış zirveleri hangi tarihlerde?

İki büyük etkinlik: Hot Sale, Mayıs sonunda bir online satış haftası, 2026'da 25 Mayıs-2 Haziran; ve Buen Fin, Kasım'da bir indirim hafta sonu, 2026'da 13-16 Kasım. 10 Mayıs Anneler Günü ve Aralık yılbaşı dönemi iki dalga daha ekler. Bunlar Meksika alışveriş takviminin kalbidir.

Hot Sale 2026 ne zaman?

Hot Sale 2026, 25 Mayıs-2 Haziran arası, dokuz gündür; Meksika'nın online satış derneği AMVO tarafından düzenlenir. Ağırlıkla dijital bir etkinlik olması e-ticaret için özellikle önemli kılar; yüzlerce satıcı elektronik, moda, ev ve seyahatte indirim sunar.

Buen Fin 2026 ne zaman?

Buen Fin 2026, 13-16 Kasım arası, 16 Kasım Devrim Günü tatiline bağlı uzun bir hafta sonudur. Meksika'nın en büyük genel indirim etkinliğidir, hem online hem fiziksel perakendeyi kapsar ve Hacienda, Concanaco ile Profeco tarafından birlikte düzenlenir.

Türk satıcı stoğunu ne zaman hazır etmeli?

Diğerlerinden daha erken, çünkü mal Türkiye'den ABD üzerinden Meksika'ya uzun bir yol kat eder. Buen Fin için yaz sonu-Eylül'de, Hot Sale için ilkbahar başında Türkiye'den yola çıkmış olmalı ki gümrüklenip ülkede hazır olsun. Etkinlik sırasında hâlâ yolda olan stok zirveyi kaçırır.

Tarihler her yıl değişir mi?

Biraz, evet. Buen Fin 16 Kasım hafta sonuna, Hot Sale ise Mayıs sonuna bağlıdır, ama kesin tarihleri her yıl AMVO ve Buen Fin için Hacienda, Concanaco, Profeco belirler. Resmi tarihleri yıllık teyit et; stoğu önceden hazır etme disiplini değişmez.

translations.title: Is Nearshoring To Mexico Cheaper Than Importing From China?
translations.excerpt: China often wins on unit price, but nearshoring to Mexico can win on total landed cost once you add freight, tariffs, lead time and inventory. Here is the honest 2026 comparison.
translations.content:
It depends on which cost you measure. China often has the lower unit price, but nearshoring to Mexico frequently wins on total landed cost once you add ocean freight, tariff exposure, long lead times and the inventory you must carry to cover them. US-origin and Mexican-origin goods can enter the US duty-free under USMCA, while Chinese goods face elevated US tariffs, and Mexico is days from the border rather than weeks. Compare the total cost of ownership, not the sticker price.

  • China usually has a lower unit (factory) price, but that is only one line of the total landed cost.
  • Chinese goods face elevated US tariffs in 2026, while USMCA-qualifying Mexican goods can enter the US duty-free (USTR, CBP).
  • Ocean freight from China runs weeks; Mexico is 1 to 2 days from the border by truck, which cuts lead time and the inventory you carry (operations data).
  • Shorter lead times let you hold less safety stock, lowering inventory carrying cost and the risk of overordering.
  • The honest answer is a total-cost comparison, not a unit-price one; the right sourcing depends on your product and volumes.

Is nearshoring to Mexico actually cheaper than China?

On unit price, usually no; China often makes it cheaper at the factory. On total landed cost, frequently yes, because Mexico avoids the elevated US tariffs Chinese goods face, ships in days instead of weeks, and lets you carry less inventory. The right answer is a total-cost comparison for your specific product.

The honest answer to whether nearshoring to Mexico is cheaper than China is that it depends on which cost you are measuring, and the mistake most buyers make is comparing only the unit price. On the factory unit price, China often still wins, thanks to scale and a mature supplier base, and for some products that gap is real. But the unit price is one line of the total landed cost, and the other lines increasingly favor Mexico. First, tariffs: in 2026 Chinese goods face elevated US tariffs, while goods that qualify as Mexican-origin under USMCA can enter the US duty-free, which can erase or reverse the unit-price advantage on many products. Second, freight and lead time: ocean shipping from China takes weeks and is exposed to port and rate volatility, while Mexico is 1 to 2 days from the US border by truck, so the goods arrive far faster and more predictably. Third, inventory: long lead times force you to carry more safety stock to avoid running out, which ties up cash and raises the risk of overordering products that then do not sell, whereas short lead times let you order closer to demand and hold less. When you add these lines, the comparison often flips, and nearshoring wins on total cost even when China wins on unit price. The point is not that Mexico is always cheaper, but that the sticker price is the wrong test. BringGo Ship supports the Mexico side of a nearshoring move, importing and distributing from the border so the total-cost advantages, speed and low inventory, are real.

What goes into the total cost of ownership?

Five lines: the unit price, the freight, the tariffs and duties, the lead time, and the inventory you must carry to cover that lead time. China tends to win the first line and Mexico the rest, so add all five for your product rather than comparing factory prices alone.

Comparing sourcing options fairly means adding up the total cost of ownership, and it has five main lines that behave very differently for China and Mexico. The first is the unit price, the factory cost of the goods, where China often leads. The second is freight: ocean and inland from China across the Pacific versus a truck from Mexico across the border, where Mexico is cheaper per unit of time and far faster. The third is tariffs and duties: this is where the picture has shifted, because Chinese goods face elevated US tariffs in 2026 while USMCA-qualifying Mexican goods can enter the US duty-free, a difference that can be larger than the unit-price gap. The fourth is lead time, which is a cost even though it does not appear on an invoice: weeks of transit from China means weeks of your cash sitting in transit and weeks of demand you must forecast in advance, versus days from Mexico. The fifth is inventory carrying cost: long, uncertain lead times force you to hold more safety stock, which ties up cash, incurs storage and, worst of all, raises the risk of buying the wrong products in bulk, while short lead times let you reorder closer to real demand and carry less. Add these five lines for your specific product and volume, and you get the real answer, which is often different from the factory-price comparison. The lines that favor Mexico, tariffs, freight, lead time and inventory, are exactly the ones a border operation strengthens. BringGo Ship runs the Mexican side of that operation, keeping freight, lead time and inventory low.

When does nearshoring to Mexico make the most sense?

When lead time, tariff exposure or inventory risk matter most: products with volatile demand, short life cycles, heavy US tariffs, or a need for fast replenishment. When unit price dominates and demand is stable, China may still win. Many buyers use both, nearshoring the volatile and tariff-heavy lines.

Nearshoring to Mexico makes the most sense for the products where the lines beyond unit price matter most, and knowing which those are turns the total-cost idea into a decision. It makes the strongest case when lead time is critical, for products with volatile or fast-changing demand, short life cycles or seasonality, where being able to reorder in days rather than weeks prevents both stockouts and overstock. It makes a strong case when tariff exposure is high, for products that face heavy US tariffs from China but can qualify as Mexican-origin under USMCA and enter duty-free, since there the tariff line alone can decide it. And it makes sense when inventory risk is expensive, for high-value goods where carrying weeks of safety stock ties up serious cash. On the other side, China may still win when the unit price dominates the total, demand is stable and predictable, and the product faces little tariff exposure, because there the factory-price advantage is not offset. In practice, many buyers do not choose one or the other but split their sourcing, nearshoring the volatile, tariff-heavy and fast-moving lines to Mexico while keeping stable, price-sensitive lines in China, and rebalancing as tariffs and demand change. The right move is to run the total-cost comparison per product rather than as a blanket policy. For the lines you do nearshore, the Mexican side needs to deliver the speed and low inventory that justify it, which is what a border operation provides. BringGo Ship handles that side, so nearshored lines arrive fast and clear duty-free where they qualify.

China vs nearshoring to Mexico: total cost lines (2026)

Cost lineChinaMexico
Unit priceOften lowerOften higher
US tariffsElevated in 2026Duty-free (USMCA-qualifying)
Freight / lead timeWeeks by ocean1 to 2 days by truck
Inventory carriedMore safety stockLess, reorder near demand
Best forStable, price-driven linesVolatile, tariff-heavy lines

Definitions

  • Total cost of ownership: Total cost of ownership adds unit price, freight, tariffs, lead time and inventory carrying cost, rather than comparing factory prices alone.
  • Nearshoring: Nearshoring is moving sourcing or manufacturing closer to the end market, such as to Mexico for the US, to cut lead time and tariff exposure.
  • Inventory carrying cost: Inventory carrying cost is the cash, storage and risk tied up in the safety stock you hold to cover long lead times.

Frequently asked questions

Is nearshoring to Mexico cheaper than importing from China?

It depends on which cost you measure. China often has the lower unit price, but nearshoring to Mexico frequently wins on total landed cost once you add freight, elevated US tariffs on Chinese goods, long lead times and the inventory you carry. Compare the total cost of ownership for your product, not the factory sticker price.

Why can Mexico beat China on total cost even at a higher unit price?

Because the other cost lines favor Mexico: USMCA-qualifying Mexican goods enter the US duty-free while Chinese goods face elevated 2026 tariffs, Mexico ships in days rather than weeks, and short lead times let you carry less inventory. Those lines can add up to more than China's unit-price advantage.

What should I include in a China vs Mexico cost comparison?

Five lines: the unit price, the freight, the tariffs and duties, the lead time, and the inventory you must carry to cover that lead time. China tends to win the first line and Mexico the other four, so add all five for your specific product and volume rather than comparing factory prices alone.

When does nearshoring to Mexico make the most sense?

When lead time, tariff exposure or inventory risk matter most: products with volatile demand, short life cycles, heavy US tariffs from China, or a need for fast replenishment. When unit price dominates and demand is stable with little tariff exposure, China may still win. Many buyers split, nearshoring the volatile, tariff-heavy lines.

Do I have to choose China or Mexico for everything?

No. In practice many buyers split their sourcing, nearshoring the fast-moving, tariff-heavy and volatile lines to Mexico while keeping stable, price-sensitive lines in China, and rebalancing as tariffs and demand shift. The decision is best made per product with a total-cost comparison, not as a blanket policy.

  • US Customs and Border Protection (cbp.gov)
  • Secretaría de Economía (FDI data) (gob.mx)

translations.title: Shipping Cosmetics To Mexico: COFEPRIS Rules Explained
translations.excerpt: Cosmetics do not need pre-market registration in Mexico, but they are far from unregulated: a COFEPRIS Notice of Operation, NOM-141 Spanish labeling and a local responsible party are the real requirements.
translations.content:
Cosmetics do not need a prior sanitary registration to enter and sell in Mexico, unlike medicines, but they are not unregulated. The importing establishment must have a COFEPRIS Notice of Operation (Aviso de Funcionamiento), the product must carry Spanish labeling under NOM-141, a local Mexican responsible party must handle the regulatory and import side, and the formula must comply with Mexico's allowed and prohibited ingredient lists. As of mid-2026, COFEPRIS has moved to digitize and speed up these procedures.

  • Cosmetics in Mexico do NOT require a prior sanitary registration (registro sanitario) to be imported and sold, unlike drugs (COFEPRIS, General Health Law).
  • The establishment that imports, manufactures, stores or distributes cosmetics must file a COFEPRIS Notice of Operation, the Aviso de Funcionamiento (COFEPRIS).
  • Labeling must follow NOM-141-SSA1/SCFI-2012, in Spanish, on prepackaged cosmetic products (DOF).
  • Foreign firms must operate through a local Mexican entity that handles regulatory submissions, import clearance and COFEPRIS communication.
  • As of 11 July 2026, COFEPRIS published an agreement to simplify and digitize health procedures; verify the current process before shipping.

What does COFEPRIS require to import cosmetics into Mexico?

Not a product registration, but four things: a COFEPRIS Notice of Operation for the importing establishment, Spanish labeling under NOM-141, a local Mexican responsible party, and a formula that complies with Mexico's allowed and prohibited ingredient lists. Cosmetics are lighter to import than drugs, but they are not unregulated.

The most useful thing to know about shipping cosmetics to Mexico is that they sit in a middle ground: lighter than medicines, which need a full sanitary registration, but heavier than an unregulated product. Cosmetics do not require a prior sanitary registration, a registro sanitario, to be imported and sold, which surprises sellers who expect a drug-like approval. But that does not mean there are no rules; there are four that matter. First, the establishment that imports, manufactures, stores or distributes the cosmetics must have a COFEPRIS Notice of Operation, the Aviso de Funcionamiento, which is how COFEPRIS recognizes the entity as authorized for those activities. Second, the product must carry Spanish labeling that follows NOM-141, Mexico's cosmetic labeling standard, covering the identity, responsible party and required information in Spanish. Third, a foreign brand must operate through a local Mexican entity that handles the regulatory submissions, the import clearance and communication with COFEPRIS, because the responsibility sits with a party inside Mexico. Fourth, the formula must comply with Mexico's lists of allowed and prohibited substances, so a product with a banned or restricted ingredient must be reformulated before it can enter. Get these four right and cosmetics move; miss one and the shipment is at risk. A useful current note: as of mid-2026 COFEPRIS has moved to digitize and speed up its procedures, so confirm the current process before you ship. BringGo Ship handles the import and Spanish NOM labeling of cosmetics through its licensed broker so they clear correctly.

Why do cosmetics shipments get held at customs?

Almost always for the same reasons: NOM-141 labeling errors, wrong tariff classification, or missing importer and responsible-party details. Cosmetics are not held because they lack a registration they never needed, but because the label, the classification or the establishment paperwork is wrong.

Understanding why cosmetics get detained is the fastest way to avoid it, because the causes are consistent and preventable. The single most common reason is labeling: NOM-141 requires specific information in Spanish on prepackaged cosmetics, and an incomplete, incorrect or English-only label is one of the top causes of customs detention, often leading to the goods being returned or destroyed. The second is classification: cosmetics must be declared under the correct tariff classification, and a misclassified product can be held while customs resolves it. The third is establishment and responsible-party details: because the Notice of Operation and a local responsible party are what authorize the activity, missing or inconsistent importer details raise a flag. Notice what is not on this list: a missing sanitary registration, because cosmetics do not need one, so sellers who worry about registration while ignoring the label are worrying about the wrong thing. The practical lesson is that cosmetics clear when the label is correct in Spanish under NOM-141, the classification is right, and the importing establishment and responsible party are in order, and they are held when one of these is wrong. This is exactly the kind of preparation that belongs before the goods cross, at a border warehouse, rather than being discovered at customs. BringGo Ship prepares the Spanish NOM-141 labeling and clears cosmetics with the correct classification and importer details through its licensed broker, which is what keeps them from being held.

How do you ship cosmetics to Mexico cleanly?

Set up a local responsible party with a COFEPRIS Notice of Operation, confirm your formula against Mexico's ingredient lists, apply compliant NOM-141 Spanish labeling before the goods cross, and clear with the correct classification. Because COFEPRIS is digitizing its processes in 2026, verify the current requirements before each shipment.

Shipping cosmetics to Mexico cleanly is a sequence, and following it in order is what keeps the goods moving. First, establish the regulatory base: operate through a local Mexican entity that holds a COFEPRIS Notice of Operation for importing cosmetics, since a foreign brand cannot carry that responsibility itself. Second, check your formula: compare your ingredients against Mexico's allowed and prohibited substance lists, and reformulate anything non-compliant before you commit to importing, because an ingredient problem is far cheaper to fix before shipping than at the border. Third, prepare the labeling: apply NOM-141-compliant Spanish labeling to the prepackaged products before they cross, covering the required information, because labeling is the top cause of detention. Fourth, clear correctly: declare the cosmetics under the correct tariff classification with a licensed broker, paying the 16 percent IVA and, for US-origin goods with a certificate of origin, clearing duty-free under USMCA. Fifth, and important for 2026, verify the current process: COFEPRIS published an agreement in July 2026 to simplify and digitize its health procedures, so the exact steps and timelines may be changing, and confirming them before each shipment avoids surprises. Done in this order, cosmetics move without the holds, returns or destruction that catch unprepared sellers. The work that matters most, the labeling, the classification and the import, is best done at a border warehouse before the goods cross. BringGo Ship performs that preparation and clearance so cosmetics arrive compliant and ready to sell.

Cosmetics into Mexico: what COFEPRIS requires (2026)

RequirementNeeded?Note
Sanitary registrationNoUnlike drugs, not required
Notice of Operation (Aviso)YesFor the importing establishment
NOM-141 Spanish labelingYesTop cause of holds if wrong
Local responsible partyYesForeign firms operate through it
Ingredient complianceYesAllowed / prohibited lists

Definitions

  • Aviso de Funcionamiento: The Aviso de Funcionamiento is the COFEPRIS Notice of Operation that authorizes an establishment to import, store or distribute cosmetics in Mexico.
  • NOM-141: NOM-141 is Mexico's labeling standard for cosmetics, requiring specific information in Spanish on prepackaged products.
  • COFEPRIS: COFEPRIS is Mexico's health-risk protection authority, which regulates cosmetics under the General Health Law and can inspect for compliance.

Frequently asked questions

What does COFEPRIS require to import cosmetics into Mexico?

Not a product registration, but four things: a COFEPRIS Notice of Operation (Aviso de Funcionamiento) for the importing establishment, Spanish labeling under NOM-141, a local Mexican responsible party, and a formula that complies with Mexico's allowed and prohibited ingredient lists. Cosmetics are lighter to import than drugs, but not unregulated.

Do cosmetics need a sanitary registration in Mexico?

No. Unlike medicines, cosmetics do not require a prior sanitary registration to be imported and sold in Mexico. Sellers who worry about registration often overlook the real requirements: the Notice of Operation, NOM-141 Spanish labeling, a local responsible party and ingredient compliance.

Why do cosmetics shipments get held at customs?

Almost always for NOM-141 labeling errors, wrong tariff classification, or missing importer and responsible-party details. Cosmetics are not held for lacking a registration they never needed; they are held when the Spanish label, the classification or the establishment paperwork is wrong. Preparing these before the goods cross avoids it.

Can a foreign brand import cosmetics into Mexico directly?

Not on its own. A foreign firm must operate through a local Mexican entity that holds the COFEPRIS Notice of Operation and handles the regulatory submissions, import clearance and communication with COFEPRIS. The responsibility for the product sits with a party inside Mexico.

Did the rules for cosmetics change in 2026?

The framework, no registration but a Notice of Operation, NOM-141 labeling and a responsible party, is stable, but as of 11 July 2026 COFEPRIS published an agreement to simplify and digitize its health procedures, aiming for faster deadlines. Because it is recent, verify the current process and timelines before each shipment.

  • DOF (NOM-141) (dof.gob.mx)
  • SAT (Mexican tax authority) (sat.gob.mx)

translations.title: Do Returned Goods From Mexico Need A Customs Declaration?
translations.excerpt: A return that crosses the border again is a customs event, and it needs a declaration. Here is when returned goods from Mexico need paperwork, and how an in-country returns hub avoids most of it.
translations.content:
Yes, if the returned goods cross the border again. A product going back from Mexico to the US is a customs event on both sides: it is re-exported from Mexico and re-imported into the US, so it needs the corresponding declarations. If instead you handle the return inside Mexico, inspecting and restocking it locally, it never re-crosses the border and avoids that customs paperwork entirely, which is why an in-country returns hub is cheaper.

  • A return that crosses the border again is a customs event and requires a declaration on the side it enters.
  • Sending a return from Mexico back to the US means re-export from Mexico plus re-import into the US, each with its own paperwork and cost.
  • Handling the return inside Mexico, inspecting and restocking locally, avoids re-crossing the border and its customs declarations.
  • Only genuinely necessary returns should be consolidated and shipped back together, spreading the fixed customs and freight cost across many units.
  • US goods returned to the US may qualify for duty relief provisions, but they still require the correct declaration and documentation (CBP).

Do returned goods from Mexico need a customs declaration?

Yes, whenever they cross the border again. A product returning from Mexico to the US is re-exported from Mexico and re-imported into the US, so each side needs its declaration and documentation. A return that stays inside Mexico, handled at a local hub, does not re-cross and avoids that paperwork.

The short answer is that returned goods need a customs declaration whenever they cross the border again, because a border crossing is a customs event regardless of whether the goods are new or returned. If a Mexican customer returns a product and you send it back to the US, that single return is actually two customs events: it is re-exported out of Mexico, and it is re-imported into the US, and each side requires the corresponding declaration and documentation. That is real paperwork and real cost, and for most individual returns it is more than the product is worth, which is the core problem with cross-border returns. The important nuance is the word again: goods only need this declaration if they re-cross the border. If instead the return is handled inside Mexico, received at a local returns hub, inspected and restocked into your Mexican inventory, it never leaves the country and never becomes a customs event, so it needs no re-export or re-import declaration at all. This is why where you handle returns decides how much customs paperwork you face. Sending every return back across the border generates two declarations per item; handling returns in-country generates none for the ones that stay. BringGo Ship runs an in-country returns hub in Monterrey, so most returns are inspected and restocked locally without a customs declaration, and only the few that genuinely must go back are declared.

What paperwork does a cross-border return actually need?

On the Mexican side, a re-export declaration to take the goods out; on the US side, a re-import entry to bring them back, with documentation of what the goods are and that they are returns. US goods returned to the US may qualify for duty relief, but the declaration and proof are still required, which is why per-item returns are costly.

When a return genuinely has to cross the border back to the US, it is worth knowing what that involves, because the cost is what makes handling returns locally so attractive. On the Mexican side, the goods must be re-exported, which is a customs process that declares them leaving the country. On the US side, they must be re-imported, entering US customs as a return, with documentation establishing what the goods are and that they are goods being returned rather than a new commercial import. US goods that are being returned to the US may qualify for duty relief provisions designed for returned American goods, but qualifying is not automatic: it requires the correct entry and documentation proving the goods are US-origin returns, so the paperwork does not disappear, it just may avoid a second duty. The practical consequence is that each cross-border return carries a re-export, a re-import, and the documentation to support both, which for a single low-value item costs more than the item, and even in bulk is a real expense. This is exactly why the smart approach is to minimize what crosses back: handle returns inside Mexico wherever possible, and only consolidate the genuinely necessary returns, the ones that must physically go back, into a single shipment so the fixed customs and freight cost is spread across many units rather than paid per item. BringGo Ship handles both sides, inspecting and restocking most returns in Mexico and consolidating the few that must return so the declarations are filed once for the batch, not per item.

How do you avoid the returns customs cost?

Handle returns inside Mexico. A local returns hub receives, inspects and restocks resellable items into your Mexican inventory without re-crossing the border, so they need no declaration. Only accumulate and consolidate the returns that genuinely must go back to the US, filing the customs paperwork once for the whole batch.

Avoiding the customs cost of returns is mostly about geography: keep them inside Mexico, and the customs event never happens. The way to do it is an in-country returns hub. When a Mexican customer returns a product, it goes to a local address in Mexico, where it is received, inspected and graded, and if it is resellable it is restocked directly into your Mexican inventory and sold again, all without leaving the country and therefore without any re-export or re-import declaration. That single design choice removes the customs paperwork from the large majority of returns, because most returns are resellable and belong back in local stock rather than back across the border. Only a minority of returns genuinely need to go back to the US, for repair, recall or specific reasons, and for those the right approach is to accumulate them and ship them back as one consolidated batch, so the re-export and re-import declarations and the freight are filed and paid once for the whole shipment rather than per item. This is the difference between a returns process that bleeds money on customs paperwork and one that is controlled: the first sends every return back individually and pays two declarations each time, the second keeps returns local and consolidates only the exceptions. The requirement is an operator with a returns hub inside Mexico and the ability to consolidate and clear the exceptions across the border. BringGo Ship provides both, with a Monterrey returns hub for local handling and consolidated return-to-US through Laredo for the few that must go back.

Returns from Mexico: customs impact by approach

ApproachCustoms declaration?Cost
Send each return back to USRe-export + re-import, per itemHigh, more than item
Handle return inside MexicoNone, stays in countryLow, local restock
Consolidate the exceptionsOnce for the batchSpread across many units
US-origin returned to USStill declared; may get duty reliefDocumentation required

Definitions

  • Re-export declaration: A re-export declaration is the Mexican customs process for taking goods, including returns, back out of the country.
  • Returns hub: A returns hub is an in-country facility that receives, inspects and restocks returns so resellable items never re-cross the border.
  • Returned goods duty relief: Returned goods duty relief is a US provision that may avoid a second duty on US-origin goods coming back, but still requires the correct declaration.

Frequently asked questions

Do returned goods from Mexico need a customs declaration?

Yes, whenever they cross the border again. A product returning from Mexico to the US is re-exported from Mexico and re-imported into the US, so each side needs its declaration and documentation. A return handled inside Mexico, at a local hub, does not re-cross the border and needs no such declaration.

What paperwork does a cross-border return need?

A re-export declaration on the Mexican side to take the goods out, and a re-import entry on the US side to bring them back, with documentation of what the goods are and that they are returns. US-origin goods may qualify for duty relief on return, but the declaration and proof are still required.

How do I avoid the customs cost of returns?

Handle returns inside Mexico. A local returns hub receives, inspects and restocks resellable items into your Mexican inventory without re-crossing the border, so they need no declaration. Only accumulate and consolidate the returns that genuinely must go back to the US, filing the paperwork once for the whole batch.

Can returned US goods come back without paying duty again?

Possibly. US-origin goods being returned to the US may qualify for returned-goods duty relief provisions, but qualifying is not automatic: it requires the correct entry and documentation proving the goods are US-origin returns. So the declaration is still needed even if a second duty is avoided.

Is it cheaper to restock returns in Mexico than send them back?

Almost always, yes. Restocking a resellable return into local Mexican inventory avoids the re-export and re-import declarations and the international freight, which for most items cost more than the product. Sending returns back individually pays two declarations each time; a local hub avoids that for everything that stays in country.

Handle Mexican returns locally with BringGo Ship's returns hub

  • SAT (Mexican tax authority) (sat.gob.mx)
  • Profeco (consumer protection) (gob.mx)

translations.title: Bonded Warehouse Vs FTZ In Laredo: Which Fits Your Inventory Strategy
translations.excerpt: A bonded warehouse and an FTZ both let you hold imported goods without paying US duty up front, but they work differently. Here is how they compare for Mexico-bound inventory in Laredo.
translations.content:
A bonded warehouse and a Foreign Trade Zone (FTZ) both let you hold imported goods in Laredo without paying US duty up front, but they differ in the details. A bonded warehouse defers duty for a limited period, typically up to five years, and requires a customs bond. An FTZ treats the goods as outside US customs territory with no time limit and no per-entry bond, and duty is avoided entirely if the goods are re-exported. For Mexico-bound inventory that is only transiting the US, the FTZ's re-export benefit usually fits best.

  • Both a bonded warehouse and an FTZ let you hold imported goods without paying US duty on arrival (US Customs, FTZ Board).
  • A bonded warehouse defers duty for a limited period, commonly up to five years, and requires a customs bond.
  • An FTZ treats goods as outside US customs territory, with no time limit and no per-entry bond; duty is avoided on re-export.
  • For Mexico-bound inventory that is re-exported rather than entering US commerce, the FTZ avoids US duty entirely.
  • US-origin goods entering Mexico still clear duty-free under USMCA with a certificate of origin regardless of which option you use (SAT, USTR).

Bonded warehouse or FTZ in Laredo: which should I use?

For Mexico-bound inventory that is only transiting the US, an FTZ usually fits best, because it avoids US duty entirely on re-export, has no time limit and needs no per-entry bond. A bonded warehouse fits when you want simple duty deferral for a limited period on goods that may enter US commerce, with a customs bond.

Both a bonded warehouse and an FTZ solve the same basic problem, holding imported goods without paying US duty up front, so choosing between them comes down to the details, and for Mexico-bound inventory those details point one way. A bonded warehouse is a US Customs-approved facility where you can store imported goods with duty deferred, but the deferral is time-limited, commonly up to five years, and the arrangement requires a customs bond. It suits goods you want to hold before deciding to enter them into US commerce, where you simply want to postpone the duty. An FTZ, a Foreign Trade Zone, is different in kind: the goods are treated as if they were outside US customs territory even while physically inside the country, there is no time limit on how long they can stay, and no per-entry bond in the same way. Crucially, if the goods are re-exported rather than entered into US commerce, the US duty is avoided entirely, not just deferred. For inventory that is Mexico-bound, only transiting the US on its way across the border, that re-export benefit is exactly the point, which is why an FTZ usually fits Mexico-bound goods better than a bonded warehouse. The choice, then, is less about which is better in general and more about what your goods are doing: transiting to Mexico favors the FTZ, holding before possible US entry favors the bonded warehouse. BringGo Ship operates on the Laredo border where both structures are available and stages Mexico-bound inventory to fit whichever saves the most.

How do bonded warehouses and FTZs differ in practice?

On four points: time limit (bonded up to five years, FTZ none), bond (bonded requires one, FTZ works differently), duty on re-export (bonded still owes it unless re-exported per rules, FTZ avoids it), and value-added activity (FTZ generally allows more manipulation like kitting and assembly). For Mexico transit, the FTZ's re-export and no-time-limit points matter most.

Seeing the differences side by side makes the choice concrete, and there are four that matter for a shipper. First, the time limit: a bonded warehouse defers duty for a limited period, commonly up to five years, after which the goods must be entered or exported, while an FTZ has no such time limit, so goods can sit as long as needed. Second, the bond: a bonded warehouse requires a customs bond as security for the deferred duty, while an FTZ operates under a different framework that does not carry the same per-entry bond. Third, and most important for Mexico-bound goods, the treatment on exit: from a bonded warehouse the duty is deferred and becomes due if the goods enter US commerce, while from an FTZ the duty is avoided entirely if the goods are re-exported, for example across the border to Mexico. Fourth, value-added activity: FTZs generally permit more manipulation of the goods, such as kitting, labeling, light assembly and repackaging, which is useful if you prepare Mexico-bound inventory in Laredo before it crosses. For a business whose goods are transiting to Mexico, the FTZ's combination of no time limit, no per-entry bond, duty avoided on re-export, and room for value-added prep is what makes it the natural fit, while a bonded warehouse remains a solid choice for straightforward duty deferral on goods that may stay in the US. One point holds for both: on the Mexican side, US-origin goods still clear duty-free under USMCA with a certificate of origin. BringGo Ship stages and prepares Mexico-bound inventory at the Laredo border using the structure that fits your goods.

Which fits your inventory strategy?

Match the structure to what your inventory does. Transiting to Mexico and want to avoid US duty plus prep the goods before crossing: FTZ. Holding goods that may enter US commerce and want simple time-limited duty deferral: bonded warehouse. For most Mexico-bound e-commerce and freight inventory, the FTZ is the better fit, but confirm against your own flow.

Deciding which fits your inventory strategy is a matter of matching the structure to what your goods actually do, and a few clear cases make it easy. If your inventory is Mexico-bound, only passing through the US on its way across the border, and especially if you want to consolidate, label or prep it in Laredo before it crosses, the FTZ is the better fit: it avoids US duty on re-export, has no time limit, and allows the value-added work. If your inventory is goods you may enter into US commerce and you simply want to postpone the duty for a while, a bonded warehouse fits, giving you time-limited deferral with a customs bond. If you hold a mix, some transiting to Mexico and some destined for the US market, you may use both, or lean on an operator that can stage each shipment in the right structure. The one thing to avoid is assuming a single answer for all inventory, because the benefit depends entirely on whether the goods re-export or enter US commerce. For most Mexico-bound e-commerce and freight inventory, the practical answer is the FTZ, given the re-export benefit and the room to prepare goods before crossing, but the right move is to confirm against your own flow rather than a general rule. And whichever you choose, remember the Mexican-side saving is separate: US-origin goods clear Mexican duty-free under USMCA. BringGo Ship provides border warehousing and clearance on the Laredo-Monterrey lane and stages your inventory in the structure that fits, so the duty benefit is real and the goods cross prepared.

Bonded warehouse vs FTZ in Laredo

FeatureBonded warehouseForeign Trade Zone (FTZ)
US duty on arrivalDeferredNot imported yet
Time limitUp to ~5 yearsNo time limit
Customs bondRequiredDifferent framework
Re-export to MexicoDeferralDuty avoided entirely
Value-added prepLimitedKitting, labeling, assembly

Definitions

  • Bonded warehouse: A bonded warehouse is a US Customs-approved facility where imported goods are stored with duty deferred for a limited period, under a customs bond.
  • Foreign Trade Zone (FTZ): An FTZ is a US site treated as outside customs territory, with no time limit and duty avoided if goods are re-exported.
  • Duty deferral: Duty deferral means US import duty is postponed while goods are held, becoming due only if they enter US commerce.

Frequently asked questions

Bonded warehouse or FTZ in Laredo: which should I use?

For Mexico-bound inventory that is only transiting the US, an FTZ usually fits best, because it avoids US duty entirely on re-export, has no time limit and needs no per-entry bond. A bonded warehouse fits when you want simple, time-limited duty deferral on goods that may enter US commerce, with a customs bond.

What is the difference between a bonded warehouse and an FTZ?

Four points: time limit (bonded up to about five years, FTZ none), bond (bonded requires one, FTZ works differently), duty on re-export (bonded defers it, FTZ avoids it entirely), and value-added activity (FTZs generally allow more manipulation like kitting and assembly). For Mexico transit, the FTZ's re-export and no-time-limit points matter most.

Which is better for Mexico-bound inventory?

Usually the FTZ, because inventory transiting to Mexico is re-exported rather than entering US commerce, so the FTZ avoids the US duty entirely and lets you prep the goods before crossing. A bonded warehouse is better for straightforward duty deferral on goods that may stay in the US market.

Do these change my Mexican duty?

No. Both address US duty, not Mexican duty. On the Mexican side, goods still clear customs and pay the 16 percent IVA, and US-origin goods with a valid certificate of origin clear Mexican duty-free under USMCA regardless of whether they passed through a bonded warehouse or an FTZ.

Can I prepare goods before they cross in these facilities?

More so in an FTZ. FTZs generally permit value-added activity such as kitting, labeling, light assembly and repackaging, which is useful for preparing Mexico-bound inventory in Laredo before it crosses. Bonded warehouses are more limited in the manipulation they allow, focusing on storage with deferred duty.

Stage Mexico-bound inventory in the right structure with BringGo Ship

Sources

  • US Foreign-Trade Zones Board (trade.gov)
  • US Customs and Border Protection (cbp.gov)
  • SAT (Mexican tax authority) (sat.gob.mx)

Note: This content is for general information only and is not legal, tax or customs advice. Rates and rules can change often in 2026; verify the current details with an official source (SAT, DOF, CBP) or our licensed customs broker before acting.

translations.title: Nearshoring En Nuevo León: Por Qué Monterrey Concentra La Ola
translations.excerpt: Nuevo León y Monterrey concentran buena parte de la ola de nearshoring en México. Por qué, qué lo sostiene y cómo aprovecharlo en tu logística transfronteriza.
translations.content:
Nuevo León, con Monterrey como centro, concentra buena parte de la ola de nearshoring en México por tres razones: su cercanía a la frontera de Laredo, el puerto terrestre más transitado de Norteamérica; su base industrial y de talento consolidada; y su red de parques industriales y logística. Para una empresa, esto significa que operar cerca de Monterrey acerca la producción y el almacén al mercado estadounidense, con tránsitos de 1 a 2 días a la frontera.

  • Monterrey, en Nuevo León, está a pocas horas de Laredo, el puerto terrestre más transitado de Norteamérica (WorldCity, Census).
  • México registró una inversión extranjera directa récord de unos 40,9 mil millones de dólares en 2025, aunque cerca del 68 por ciento fue reinversión de utilidades, no plantas nuevas (Secretaría de Economía).
  • Nuevo León es de los estados que más concentra la inversión ligada al nearshoring, por su base industrial y su cercanía a la frontera.
  • Un TIR documentado llega de Laredo a Monterrey en 1 a 2 días hábiles (dato operativo).
  • La mercancía de origen estadounidense entra sin arancel bajo el T-MEC con certificado de origen; el IVA de importación es 16 por ciento (SAT, USTR).

¿Por qué Nuevo León lidera el nearshoring?

Por tres razones que se refuerzan: su cercanía a la frontera de Laredo, el puerto terrestre más transitado de Norteamérica; su base industrial y de talento ya consolidada; y su red de parques industriales y logística. Juntas hacen de Monterrey un lugar natural para acercar producción y almacén al mercado estadounidense.

Nuevo León, con Monterrey como su corazón, concentra buena parte de la ola de nearshoring en México, y las razones no son una sola sino tres que se refuerzan entre sí. La primera es la geografía: Monterrey está a pocas horas de manejo de Laredo, el puerto terrestre más transitado de Norteamérica, por donde cruza cerca del 40 por ciento del comercio entre México y Estados Unidos. Estar así de cerca de la frontera más activa significa tránsitos cortos y acceso directo al mercado estadounidense, algo que ningún otro polo iguala con la misma facilidad. La segunda es la base industrial: Nuevo León tiene una tradición manufacturera de décadas, proveedores, talento técnico y empresas ancla ya instaladas, de modo que una compañía que llega no empieza de cero sino que se conecta a un ecosistema existente. La tercera es la infraestructura: una red madura de parques industriales, almacenes y servicios logísticos que permite operar producción y distribución sin construir todo desde el principio. Vale una nota honesta sobre las cifras: México registró una inversión extranjera directa récord en 2025, cerca de 40,9 mil millones de dólares, pero buena parte fue reinversión de utilidades de empresas ya presentes más que plantas nuevas, así que el nearshoring es una tendencia real y sostenida más que una explosión repentina, y Nuevo León está entre los estados que más la concentran. Para una empresa, la conclusión es que operar cerca de Monterrey acerca todo al mercado estadounidense. BringGo Ship opera justo en este corredor, con almacén en Monterrey y en Laredo.

¿Qué significa Monterrey para tu logística transfronteriza?

Que tu producción o tu almacén quedan a 1 o 2 días de la frontera. Un almacén en Monterrey te da distribución nacional dentro de México y, a la vez, un acceso rápido a Laredo para cruzar hacia Estados Unidos o recibir mercancía de allá, todo en un tránsito de 1 a 2 días hábiles.

La ventaja de Nuevo León se vuelve concreta en la logística, porque estar cerca de Monterrey cambia los tiempos y los costos de mover mercancía. Un almacén o una operación en Monterrey queda a 1 o 2 días hábiles de la frontera de Laredo, y eso funciona en las dos direcciones. Hacia adentro, la mercancía que importas de Estados Unidos cruza por Laredo y llega a tu almacén de Monterrey en 1 a 2 días, lista para distribuirse a todo México, así que tu punto de entrada al país y tu centro de distribución están prácticamente pegados. Hacia afuera, si produces o consolidas en Monterrey para vender en Estados Unidos, la frontera está a un tránsito corto, lo que acorta el ciclo hacia el mercado estadounidense frente a operar desde el centro o el sur del país. A esto se suma el beneficio arancelario: la mercancía de origen estadounidense entra a México sin arancel bajo el T-MEC con certificado de origen, y pagas el 16 por ciento de IVA sobre el valor en aduana, de modo que el costo fiscal es previsible. En la práctica, Monterrey te da lo mejor de dos mundos: distribución nacional dentro de México y una puerta rápida a Estados Unidos, con la densidad de transportistas y servicios que trae ser un polo consolidado. Por eso muchas empresas que hacen nearshoring eligen Nuevo León no solo para producir, sino para almacenar y distribuir. BringGo Ship aprovecha esta posición con su almacén en Monterrey y su operación en Laredo, para que tu mercancía se mueva rápido en ambos sentidos.

¿Cómo aprovechas la ola sin construir todo?

Apóyate en la infraestructura que ya existe: usa un operador con almacén en Monterrey y en Laredo, agente aduanal y despacho, en lugar de montar tu propia operación transfronteriza. Así accedes a las ventajas de Nuevo León, cercanía, tránsitos cortos, red logística, sin la inversión de construirla desde cero.

La mejor forma de aprovechar la ola de nearshoring en Nuevo León es apoyarte en lo que ya está construido, en lugar de replicar toda una operación transfronteriza por tu cuenta. La razón es que las ventajas de Monterrey, la cercanía a Laredo, los tránsitos cortos, la red de parques y servicios, ya existen y están disponibles a través de operadores instalados, así que una empresa no necesita comprar almacenes en ambos lados de la frontera ni contratar su propio agente aduanal para capturarlas. El camino práctico es usar un operador transfronterizo que ya tenga almacén en Monterrey y en Laredo, agente aduanal con patente y despacho, de modo que tu mercancía consolide en Laredo, cruce despachada, y se almacene y distribuya desde Monterrey, todo dentro de la misma cadena. Eso te da las economías de escala de una operación consolidada y la experiencia de un equipo que hace esto a diario, sin la inversión de construirla. Para las empresas más grandes que sí instalan producción propia en Nuevo León, un operador logístico sigue siendo el socio que mueve la mercancía entre la planta, la frontera y el mercado. Y para las PyMEs o los vendedores de e-commerce, es la manera de operar cerca de Monterrey sin ser una gran industria. La idea central es que la ola de nearshoring no exige que construyas la infraestructura, sino que te conectes a ella. BringGo Ship es esa infraestructura en el corredor Laredo-Monterrey: almacén en ambos lados, despacho y distribución, lista para que aproveches la posición de Nuevo León.

Por qué Nuevo León concentra el nearshoring

FactorQué aportaEfecto logístico
Cercanía a LaredoFrontera más transitadaTránsito 1 a 2 días
Base industrialProveedores y talentoEcosistema listo
Parques y logísticaInfraestructura maduraOperar sin construir todo
Doble víaMéxico y EE.UU.Distribución + puerta a la frontera
T-MECOrigen EE.UU. sin arancelCosto fiscal previsible

Definiciones

  • Nearshoring: El nearshoring es acercar producción o abastecimiento al mercado final, como a México para Estados Unidos, para acortar tiempos y aranceles.
  • Polo de nearshoring: Un polo de nearshoring es una región, como Nuevo León, que concentra inversión por su base industrial, ubicación e infraestructura.
  • Corredor Laredo-Monterrey: El corredor Laredo-Monterrey conecta la frontera más transitada con el centro industrial del noreste en un tránsito de 1 a 2 días.

Preguntas frecuentes

¿Por qué Nuevo León lidera el nearshoring?

Por tres razones que se refuerzan: su cercanía a la frontera de Laredo, el puerto terrestre más transitado de Norteamérica; su base industrial y de talento ya consolidada; y su red de parques industriales y logística. Juntas hacen de Monterrey un lugar natural para acercar producción y almacén al mercado estadounidense.

¿Qué significa Monterrey para mi logística?

Que tu producción o tu almacén quedan a 1 o 2 días de la frontera de Laredo. Un almacén en Monterrey te da distribución nacional dentro de México y, a la vez, acceso rápido a Laredo para cruzar hacia Estados Unidos o recibir mercancía de allá, todo en un tránsito de 1 a 2 días hábiles.

¿La inversión de nearshoring en México es tan grande como se dice?

Es real y sostenida, pero conviene leerla bien. México registró una inversión extranjera directa récord en 2025, cerca de 40,9 mil millones de dólares, pero buena parte fue reinversión de utilidades de empresas ya presentes más que plantas nuevas. Así que es una tendencia firme, no una explosión repentina, y Nuevo León está entre los estados que más la concentran.

¿Cómo aprovecho la ola sin construir toda una operación?

Apóyate en la infraestructura que ya existe: usa un operador con almacén en Monterrey y en Laredo, agente aduanal y despacho, en lugar de montar tu propia operación transfronteriza. Así accedes a la cercanía, los tránsitos cortos y la red logística de Nuevo León sin la inversión de construirla desde cero.

¿Sirve Nuevo León tanto para importar como para exportar?

Sí, funciona en las dos direcciones. La mercancía que importas de EE.UU. cruza por Laredo y llega a Monterrey en 1 a 2 días para distribuir en México; y si produces o consolidas en Monterrey para vender en EE.UU., la frontera está a un tránsito corto. La mercancía de origen estadounidense entra a México sin arancel bajo el T-MEC.

Aprovecha la posición de Monterrey con el corredor de BringGo Ship

  • WorldCity, datos de Laredo (worldcity.com)
  • SAT (autoridad fiscal de México) (sat.gob.mx)

Nota: Este contenido es solo informativo y no constituye asesoría legal, fiscal ni aduanera. Las tarifas y reglas pueden cambiar con frecuencia en 2026; verifica los datos vigentes con una fuente oficial (SAT, DOF, CBP) o con nuestro agente aduanal antes de actuar.

translations.title: NOM-004: Etiquetado De Textiles Y Ropa Importada
translations.excerpt: La NOM-004 define la etiqueta obligatoria de textiles y ropa en México. Composición, cuidado, talla, responsable y país de origen, todo en español. Aquí lo que exige, punto por punto.
translations.content:
La NOM-004 exige que los textiles y la ropa importados a México lleven, en español, cinco datos clave: la composición de fibras por porcentaje en orden descendente, las instrucciones de cuidado, la talla o dimensiones, el nombre y RFC del responsable en México, y el país de origen. En prendas, la etiqueta principal debe ser permanente y estar cosida. Sin esta etiqueta, la mercancía se detiene en la aduana.

  • La NOM-004-SCFI rige la información comercial de textiles, ropa y textiles del hogar importados y vendidos en México (DOF).
  • Exige, en español: composición de fibras por nombre genérico y porcentaje en peso, en orden descendente.
  • Exige instrucciones de cuidado, talla o dimensiones, y el nombre y RFC del responsable (fabricante o importador) en México.
  • Exige el país de origen con la leyenda Hecho en, y en prendas la etiqueta principal debe ser permanente y cosida.
  • Sin etiqueta NOM-004 correcta, la mercancía textil se detiene en la aduana; prepararla antes de cruzar lo evita.

¿Qué etiqueta exige la NOM-004 en textiles?

Cinco datos en español: composición de fibras por porcentaje en orden descendente, instrucciones de cuidado, talla o dimensiones, nombre y RFC del responsable en México, y país de origen. En prendas, la etiqueta principal debe ser permanente y cosida. Es la información comercial mínima para importar y vender textiles.

La NOM-004 es la norma que define qué debe decir la etiqueta de los textiles, la ropa y los textiles del hogar que se importan y venden en México, y cumplirla es lo que separa una importación que fluye de una que se detiene. Exige cinco datos, todos en español. El primero es la composición de fibras: hay que declarar cada fibra por su nombre genérico y su porcentaje en peso, ordenados de mayor a menor, por ejemplo algodón 60 por ciento, poliéster 40 por ciento. El segundo son las instrucciones de cuidado: cómo lavar, secar y planchar la prenda, normalmente con símbolos y texto. El tercero es la talla o las dimensiones, según sea ropa o textil del hogar como sábanas o toallas. El cuarto es el responsable: el nombre y el RFC del fabricante o del importador en México, es decir, quién responde por el producto dentro del país. El quinto es el país de origen, con la leyenda Hecho en seguida del país. Además del contenido, importa la forma: en prendas de vestir, la etiqueta principal con esta información debe ser permanente y estar cosida, no ser un colgante que se quita. Reunir estos cinco datos en español, colocados correctamente, es lo que exige la NOM-004, y hacerlo antes de que la mercancía cruce evita que se detenga en la aduana. BringGo Ship prepara el etiquetado NOM en español y despacha los textiles con su agente aduanal para que entren en regla.

¿Por qué se detienen los textiles en la aduana?

Casi siempre por la etiqueta: falta un dato de la NOM-004, está en inglés en lugar de español, o la etiqueta no es permanente en prendas. También por una clasificación arancelaria incorrecta. La mercancía textil se detiene cuando la etiqueta no cumple, no por el producto en sí.

Entender por qué se detienen los textiles es la forma más rápida de evitarlo, porque las causas se repiten y son prevenibles. La más común es la etiqueta: la NOM-004 pide cinco datos específicos en español, y basta que falte uno, que la composición o el cuidado estén en inglés, o que la etiqueta en una prenda no sea permanente y cosida, para que la mercancía se retenga. La segunda causa es la colocación y permanencia: en ropa, una etiqueta colgante o removible no cumple con el requisito de etiqueta principal permanente, aunque el contenido sea correcto. La tercera es la clasificación arancelaria: un textil mal clasificado puede detenerse mientras la aduana lo resuelve. Nota lo que casi nunca es el problema: el producto en sí, porque un textil bien fabricado se detiene igual si su etiqueta no cumple. La lección práctica es que el etiquetado no es un detalle final sino el requisito que decide si la mercancía pasa, y que revisarlo contra la NOM-004 antes de cruzar, cuando corregirlo es barato, evita el costo y la demora de una retención en la frontera. Esto es exactamente el trabajo que conviene hacer en un almacén de frontera, con la mercancía y las etiquetas en el mismo lugar, en lugar de descubrir el problema en la aduana. BringGo Ship revisa y coloca el etiquetado NOM-004 en español antes de que los textiles crucen, lo que es lo que evita que se detengan.

¿Cómo preparas tus textiles para cumplir la NOM-004?

Reúne los cinco datos en español, coloca la etiqueta principal permanente y cosida en prendas, verifica la clasificación arancelaria, y hazlo antes de que la mercancía cruce, en un almacén de frontera. Un operador con etiquetado NOM y agente aduanal deja los textiles listos para entrar en regla.

Preparar los textiles para cumplir la NOM-004 es una secuencia clara, y seguirla en orden es lo que mantiene la mercancía en movimiento. Primero, reúne los cinco datos en español: la composición de fibras por porcentaje en orden descendente, las instrucciones de cuidado, la talla o dimensiones, el nombre y RFC del responsable en México, y el país de origen con la leyenda Hecho en. Segundo, cuida la forma: en prendas, la etiqueta principal con esta información debe ser permanente y cosida, así que planifica el etiquetado desde la producción o en la preparación previa al cruce, no como un pegado de último momento. Tercero, verifica la clasificación arancelaria del textil con tu agente aduanal, porque una clasificación incorrecta detiene igual que una etiqueta incompleta. Cuarto, y este es el punto que ahorra más, haz todo esto antes de que la mercancía cruce, idealmente en un almacén de frontera donde la mercancía y las etiquetas están juntas, en lugar de descubrir un problema en la aduana. Un detalle fiscal que conviene tener presente: la mercancía de origen estadounidense entra sin arancel bajo el T-MEC con certificado de origen, pero un textil de origen fuera del T-MEC paga el arancel correspondiente, y ambos pagan el 16 por ciento de IVA. Con la etiqueta lista y la clasificación correcta, los textiles entran sin las retenciones que atrapan a los vendedores no preparados. BringGo Ship realiza esta preparación y el despacho, para que tus textiles crucen etiquetados en regla.

NOM-004: los datos obligatorios en la etiqueta

DatoQué incluyeNota
Composición de fibrasNombre y % en pesoOrden descendente
CuidadoLavado, secado, planchadoSímbolos y texto
Talla / dimensionesRopa o textil del hogarSegún el producto
ResponsableNombre y RFC en MéxicoFabricante o importador
País de origenHecho en...En prendas, etiqueta cosida

Definiciones

  • NOM-004: La NOM-004-SCFI es la norma mexicana de información comercial para textiles, ropa y textiles del hogar, que exige datos específicos en español.
  • Composición de fibras: La composición de fibras es la lista de cada fibra por nombre y porcentaje en peso, en orden descendente, que la NOM-004 exige en la etiqueta.
  • Responsable en México: El responsable en México es el fabricante o importador cuyo nombre y RFC deben aparecer en la etiqueta del textil.

Preguntas frecuentes

¿Qué etiqueta exige la NOM-004 en textiles?

Cinco datos en español: composición de fibras por porcentaje en orden descendente, instrucciones de cuidado, talla o dimensiones, nombre y RFC del responsable en México, y país de origen con la leyenda Hecho en. En prendas, la etiqueta principal debe ser permanente y cosida. Es la información comercial mínima para importar y vender textiles.

¿La etiqueta debe estar en español?

Sí. La NOM-004 exige que la información, la composición, el cuidado, la talla, el responsable y el país de origen, esté en español. Una etiqueta en inglés, aunque tenga todos los datos, no cumple y es una de las causas más comunes de que la mercancía textil se detenga en la aduana.

¿Por qué se detienen los textiles en la aduana?

Casi siempre por la etiqueta: falta un dato de la NOM-004, está en inglés en lugar de español, o en prendas la etiqueta no es permanente y cosida. También por una clasificación arancelaria incorrecta. El producto en sí rara vez es el problema; se detiene cuando la etiqueta no cumple.

¿La etiqueta de ropa tiene que ser cosida?

Sí, la etiqueta principal. En prendas de vestir, la etiqueta con la información de la NOM-004 debe ser permanente y estar cosida, no un colgante removible. Una etiqueta colgante no cumple con el requisito de etiqueta principal permanente, aunque el contenido sea correcto.

¿Cómo preparo mis textiles para cumplir la NOM-004?

Reúne los cinco datos en español, coloca la etiqueta principal permanente y cosida en prendas, verifica la clasificación arancelaria con tu agente, y hazlo antes de que la mercancía cruce, en un almacén de frontera. Un operador con etiquetado NOM y agente aduanal deja los textiles listos para entrar en regla.

Etiqueta tus textiles en regla con el prep y despacho de BringGo Ship

Fuentes

  • DOF (NOM-004-SCFI) (dof.gob.mx)
  • Secretaría de Economía (gob.mx)
  • SAT (autoridad fiscal de México) (sat.gob.mx)

Nota: Este contenido es solo informativo y no constituye asesoría legal, fiscal ni aduanera. Las tarifas y reglas pueden cambiar con frecuencia en 2026; verifica los datos vigentes con una fuente oficial (SAT, DOF, CBP) o con nuestro agente aduanal antes de actuar.
